proposals.title: Deconstructing HbA1c via Early Tissue Damage: An Inverse Modeling Approach to Predict Glycemic Instability in Type 2 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Despite the centrality of HbA1c in diabetes management, it is a crude average that masks critical glycemic patterns like variability and hypoglycemia, which are differentially linked to specific complications. Landmark CVOTs for drugs from Sanofi, Lilly, and others have shown distinct cardiovascular and renal outcomes, suggesting heterogeneous responses even at similar HbA1c levels. This proposal addresses the critical need to move beyond HbA1c by using early complication signatures to infer past glycemic exposures and forecast future risk.
proposals.hypothesis: Early, subclinical markers of tissue damage (e.g., albuminuria, cardiac autonomic dysfunction, or retinal vascular geometry) contain latent information about the historical glycemic patterns that caused them, enabling the stratification of patients with identical HbA1c into distinct phenotypes with different risks for imminent glycemic instability and complication progression.
proposals.method: We will analyze longitudinal data from cohorts like DPPOS, enriched with CGM data (e.g., from Abbott systems) and complication assessments. Using an inverse modeling framework, we will treat early complication vectors as outputs to infer latent historical glycemic patterns (e.g., nocturnal hypoglycemia burden, glycemic variability). Machine learning algorithms, including regularized regression (LASSO) and gradient boosting (XGBoost), will be used to map complication signatures to CGM-derived metrics and to build predictive models for future glycemic instability (defined as HbA1c increase >0.5% or severe hypoglycemia) over a 2-year horizon.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, complication-informed phenotyping system that explains outcome heterogeneity seen in trials like CVOT Summit reports. It will deliver a predictive tool for identifying patients at high risk for near-term loss of glycemic control, enabling targeted, pre-emptive intervention. The models will offer a biologically-grounded deconstruction of HbA1c, linking specific glycemic exposures to their early tissue effects.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in treating early complications as an 'inverse problem' to infer past glycemic history, rather than merely as outcomes. It integrates pre-2026 CGM trial data (e.g., InRange) with long-term complication data (DPPOS) to create predictive phenotypes, moving beyond correlation to mechanistic inference.

proposals.title: Beyond HbA1c: A Multi-Dimensional Glycemic Risk Score Integrating CGM Metrics Predicts Microvascular Outcomes in Type 2 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Despite its status as the gold standard, HbA1c fails to capture critical dimensions of dysglycemia, such as glycemic variability (GV) and time-in-range (TIR), which are increasingly implicated in diabetes complications. Recent CVOTs and CGM trials, like InRange, demonstrate that modern insulin analogs (e.g., Sanofi's Glargine 300 U/mL, Lilly's and Novo Nordisk's analogs) can have similar HbA1c outcomes but differing GV profiles, suggesting distinct pathophysiological risks not reflected by HbA1c alone. A composite metric is needed to better stratify patient risk and personalize treatment.
proposals.hypothesis: A composite glycemic risk score, integrating HbA1c with continuous glucose monitoring (CGM)-derived metrics of glycemic variability and time-in-hypo/hyperglycemia, will be a superior predictor of microvascular disease progression and related healthcare resource utilization compared to HbA1c alone in adults with type 2 diabetes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort analysis using linked Abbott Freestyle Libre CGM data, electronic health records, and claims data from a large US health system (2018-2024). For each patient-year, we will calculate HbA1c, coefficient of variation (CV), time below range (TBR), and time above range (TAR). Using machine learning (LASSO regression for feature selection, followed by Cox proportional hazards modeling), we will develop a weighted composite score predicting time to a composite microvascular outcome (retinopathy progression, incident albuminuria, or confirmed diabetic neuropathy). Model discrimination will be compared to an HbA1c-only model using Harrell's C-statistic.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, clinically actionable tool to move beyond HbA1c-centric treatment goals, offering a more nuanced assessment of glycemic control. It will generate evidence to support the integration of CGM metrics into routine risk stratification and value-based care models, potentially influencing future treatment guidelines and clinical trial design for diabetes therapies.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies (e.g., InRange) compare GV between treatments, this proposal innovatively integrates multiple CGM metrics with HbA1c into a single prognostic score for hard clinical outcomes, leveraging real-world data on a scale not previously applied to this specific question.

proposals.title: Causal Impact of EHR-Based Diabetes Type Misclassification: A Target Trial Emulation to Disentangle Labeling from Biological Risk
proposals.motivation: Electronic health record (EHR) algorithms for classifying type 1 (T1D) and type 2 (T2D) diabetes are widely used for research and quality metrics but are known to misclassify cases, particularly adult-onset T1D. This misclassification may lead to inappropriate treatment pathways (e.g., withholding GLP-1 RAs/SGLT2is from algorithm-T1D or delaying insulin in misclassified T1D) and confound real-world evidence studies. The clinical consequences of this 'label effect' versus the inherent 'biological effect' of the true diabetes type on complication risk are unknown.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that algorithmically misclassified individuals (true T1D labeled as T2D) will have complication risk profiles intermediate between correctly labeled T1D and phenotypically similar T2D, and that the divergence in outcomes between algorithm-T1D and gold-standard T1D cohorts will quantify the causal effect of diagnostic mislabeling on clinical outcomes.
proposals.method: We will emulate a target trial using longitudinal EHR data from a large integrated health system. We will apply a validated, rule-based T1D/T2D phenotyping algorithm to define an 'algorithm-T1D' cohort. Within this cohort, we will identify a 'gold-standard T1D' sub-cohort via confirmed autoantibody/C-peptide data. Using high-dimensional propensity score matching, we will create a third 'algorithm-T2D' cohort matched to likely misclassified cases on phenotype (age at diagnosis, BMI, insulin use timing). Primary outcomes (cardiovascular events, severe hypoglycemia, DKA, CKD progression) will be compared across cohorts using Cox models to isolate the label effect (algorithm-T1D vs. gold-standard T1D) and biological effect (gold-standard T1D vs. matched T2D).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first causally rigorous estimate of the clinical impact of diabetes phenotyping error, separating algorithmic labeling from disease biology. It will inform the interpretation of real-world evidence from EHRs and quantify risks associated with misclassification to guide improvements in clinical decision support and algorithm design.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has assessed algorithm accuracy, this study innovatively frames misclassification as a causal exposure using target trial emulation, a method applied to cardiovascular outcome trials (CVOTs) but not to diagnostic algorithms. It moves beyond validation to directly estimate the clinical harm of the label itself.

proposals.title: A Pragmatic Trial of a Complication-Aware Clinical Decision Support System to Overcome Therapeutic Inertia in Insulin Initiation for Type 2 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Clinical inertia in advancing to insulin therapy remains a major barrier to glycemic control, contributing to long-term complications. While cardiovascular outcome trials (CVOTs) have underscored the importance of complication prevention, early signs of microvascular disease progression are often under-captured in structured EHR data, weakening the impetus for treatment change. This gap between real-time complication detection and therapeutic action represents a critical, addressable failure point in diabetes care.
proposals.hypothesis: A clinical decision support (CDS) tool that integrates enhanced, real-time detection of early diabetes complications from unstructured clinical notes will be more effective at reducing therapeutic inertia and improving glycemic outcomes than a standard, rule-based reminder system for insulin initiation.
proposals.method: We will first develop and validate a natural language processing (NLP) pipeline using a pre-2026 transformer-based architecture (e.g., fine-tuned ClinicalBERT) to extract mentions of early neuropathy, retinopathy, and albuminuria from clinical notes in a large EHR database. This complication signal will be integrated with structured lab and medication data into a CDS tool that triggers for eligible patients (HbA1c >8.5% on dual oral therapy). In a pragmatic, cluster-randomized trial, primary care clinics will be assigned to receive either the complication-aware CDS or a standard HbA1c-based alert. The primary outcome will be time to insulin initiation (Lilly or Sanofi analogs).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, scalable method for extracting nuanced complication data from the EHR to create a more compelling, patient-specific case for treatment intensification. It will generate high-level evidence on whether data-driven, complication-focused nudges can effectively modify clinician behavior and improve real-world outcomes, moving beyond simple reminder systems.
proposals.novelty: The proposal uniquely fuses advanced NLP for complication phenotyping with direct intervention testing for inertia, creating a closed-loop, data-driven intervention. It moves beyond using complications merely as risk stratification variables to employing their real-time detection as the active driver for personalized clinical action.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Trajectories of Glycemic Control and Their Association with Microvascular Complication Onset in Type 1 Diabetes: A Decadal Analysis of Continuous Glucose Monitoring and Clinical Outcome Data
proposals.motivation: While landmark trials like DCCT/EDIC established the principle of glycemic legacy, the real-world, dynamic relationship between longitudinal glycemic patterns and the timing of specific complication onset remains poorly characterized. The advent of widespread continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) and second-generation basal insulins (e.g., Sanofi's Glargine 300 U/mL, Lilly/Novo Nordisk's Degludec) provides an unprecedented opportunity to move beyond HbA1c snapshots and model glycemic control as a time-varying risk factor. This is critical for personalizing surveillance and intensifying treatment during high-risk periods.
proposals.hypothesis: Distinct, empirically derived trajectories of glycemic control (e.g., stable optimal, stable suboptimal, deteriorating, improving), quantified by CGM-derived metrics beyond mean glucose, will be differentially associated with the time-to-onset and clustering of microvascular complications (retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy) over a 10-year follow-up period.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective longitudinal cohort study using data from existing registries (e.g., T1D Exchange) and linked CGM datasets (e.g., Abbott Freestyle Libre, Dexcom). Annualized glycemic profiles will be constructed using CGM metrics (mean glucose, glucose management indicator, coefficient of variation, time-in-range). Group-based trajectory modeling (GBTM), a specialized longitudinal finite mixture model, will identify distinct glycemic control trajectory groups. Time-dependent Cox proportional hazards models, with glycemic trajectory group as a time-varying covariate, will assess associations with complication onset, adjudicated via medical records, adjusting for baseline risk factors and treatments.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, dynamic taxonomy of glycemic control in T1D, linking specific deterioration patterns to complication risk. It will generate evidence to support risk-stratified, trajectory-informed monitoring schedules and inform the design of future cardiovascular and kidney outcomes trials (CVOTs) by identifying high-risk phenotypic subgroups for targeted intervention with newer therapies.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior studies using static HbA1c measures or short-term CGM, this analysis applies advanced longitudinal modeling (GBTM) to multi-year, high-frequency CGM data to define dynamic glycemic phenotypes and their specific complication timelines, moving from correlation to predictive trajectory-based risk stratification.

proposals.title: Capturing the Causal Cascade: A Mixed-Methods, Real-Time Analysis of Glycemic Control Failures in Emerging Adults with Type 1 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Emerging adults (18-25 years) with type 1 diabetes (T1D) have the highest HbA1c levels and poorest clinical outcomes of any age group, representing a critical public health challenge. Despite advanced therapies like second-generation basal insulin analogs (e.g., Sanofi's Glargine 300 U/mL, Lilly's Degludec) and continuous glucose monitors (e.g., Abbott's Freestyle Libre), glycemic control remains suboptimal. Current research, including cardiovascular outcome trials (CVOTs) and studies of glucose variability, fails to capture the dynamic, contextual, and psychological processes that drive real-world treatment failures in this population.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that glycemic control failures in emerging adults are precipitated by identifiable, time-linked sequences of contextual stressors, behavioral decisions, and physiological responses, which can be modeled to predict high-risk periods and inform personalized interventions.
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 emerging adults with T1D using CGM. For 28 days, smartphone-based ecological momentary assessment (EMA) will prompt participants 5x daily to report stress, mood, dietary intent, and insulin dosing decisions, synchronized with CGM data via API (e.g., Abbott LibreView). Passive sensing (GPS, accelerometry) will capture context. We will use time-series and multi-level hazard modeling to quantify the temporal risk of hyper/hypoglycemia following specific EMA-reported events. A subsample (n=30) will then undergo in-depth phenomenological interviews to explain the quantitative patterns.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrated, real-time model of the biopsychosocial cascade leading to glycemic excursions in emerging adults. It will move beyond static risk factors to identify modifiable, in-the-moment intervention targets. The mixed-methods design will yield a rich, actionable framework for developing context-aware digital health tools and behavioral strategies tailored to this high-risk group.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., the InRange trial) compares glucose variability between insulin analogs, this study uniquely focuses on the *causal pathways* of control failure by fusing high-frequency EMA, passive sensing, and CGM, then grounding statistical patterns in qualitative lived experience.

proposals.title: A Behavioral Nudge Intervention to Improve Glycemic Control in Young Adults with Type 1 Diabetes: The NUDGE-T1D Pilot Study
proposals.motivation: Young adults (18-25 years) with type 1 diabetes (T1D) consistently exhibit the highest HbA1c levels across all age groups, leading to elevated long-term risk of cardiovascular and microvascular outcomes. Despite advances in insulin analogs (e.g., Sanofi's Glargine 300 U/mL, Lilly's and Novo Nordisk's products) and continuous glucose monitoring (CGM, e.g., Abbott systems), glycemic outcomes remain suboptimal, indicating a critical gap in engagement with effective treatment. This gap is likely behavioral, stemming from unique life-stage challenges including transition stress, complex self-management, and social stigma, which are not addressed by pharmacotherapy alone.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured behavioral audit will identify specific, modifiable decision bottlenecks in daily diabetes management for young adults, and a subsequent pilot of digitally-delivered, theory-informed nudges targeting these bottlenecks will reduce behavioral friction and improve glycemic control, as measured by CGM-derived metrics including time-in-range and glucose variability.
proposals.method: We will conduct a mixed-methods behavioral audit via in-depth interviews and ecological momentary assessment (EMA) to map daily decision points and barriers. Using this map, we will design a suite of context-aware nudges (e.g., simplified post-meal bolus prompts, stigma-countering messaging, low-glucose alert follow-up actions) delivered via a smartphone app. The intervention will be piloted in a 12-week randomized controlled trial (RCT) in 60 young adults with T1D (HbA1c ≥8.0%), using CGM data as the primary outcome. Analysis will employ linear mixed models to assess changes in time-in-range (70-180 mg/dL) and glucose coefficient of variation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, behaviorally-characterized framework for understanding glycemic control barriers beyond clinical factors. It will yield preliminary efficacy data for a scalable, low-burden digital intervention designed to improve real-world engagement with diabetes technology and pharmacotherapy, potentially informing future large-scale behavioral RCTs integrated with CGM and insulin delivery systems.
proposals.novelty: While behavioral economics and nudge theory are established in public health (e.g., cancer prevention screening), their systematic application to identify and overcome specific daily decision bottlenecks in T1D self-care is novel. This approach moves beyond general education or motivation, targeting the 'choice architecture' of diabetes management with precision informed by a prior behavioral audit.

proposals.title: Linking Real-Time Psychosocial Barrier Dynamics to Glycemic Volatility and Early Complication Risk in High-Risk Emerging Adults with Type 1 Diabetes: A 5-Year Mixed-Methods Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Emerging adults (18-25) with Type 1 Diabetes (T1D), particularly those with high baseline HbA1c, face the highest risk for acute and long-term complications, yet their care trajectories are poorly understood. Pre-2026, while CVOTs and trials like InRange have quantified glycemic outcomes of new insulins (e.g., Sanofi's Glargine 300 U/mL, Lilly/Novo's analogs), and DPPOS has modeled lifestyle risk scores, a critical gap exists in dynamically linking the evolving psychosocial and systemic barriers of this life stage to real-world glycemic control and early complication pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, temporally dynamic patterns of self-reported barriers (e.g., instability during education/employment transitions) will predict not only higher glucose variability, as measured by continuous glucose monitoring (CGM), but also the accelerated emergence of early biomarkers for microvascular complications over a 5-year period.
proposals.method: We will recruit 150 emerging adults with T1D and HbA1c ≥9.0% at baseline. The mixed-methods design combines: 1) Intensive longitudinal data collection via smartphone-delivered Ecological Momentary Assessments (EMA) 3x/day and quarterly in-depth interviews to capture barrier profiles. 2) High-frequency glycemic measurement using Abbott Freestyle Libre CGM systems. 3) Annual collection of early complication biomarkers (e.g., urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio, retinal imaging). We will employ time-varying effect modeling and group-based trajectory modeling to link barrier patterns to CGM-derived metrics (e.g., %TIR, GV) and biomarker progression, controlling for treatment factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will generate the first longitudinal, integrated model of how real-world barrier dynamics directly drive glycemic trajectories and early complication risk in the highest-risk T1D population. It will identify modifiable intervention targets for life-stage-specific support, moving beyond static risk factors to dynamic, person-centered prediction.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of intensive qualitative barrier profiling with high-frequency CGM and complication biomarker data over 5+ years in a precisely defined high-risk cohort represents a novel, mechanistic approach to understanding poor outcomes, distinct from prior pharmaco-centric or cross-sectional psychosocial studies.

proposals.title: A Mixed-Methods Analysis of Psychosocial, Behavioral, and Treatment-Related Barriers to Glycemic Control in Young Adults (18-25) with Type 1 Diabetes: The YOUTH-CONTROL Study
proposals.motivation: Young adults (18-25) with type 1 diabetes (T1D) consistently exhibit the highest HbA1c levels across all age groups, placing them at significant risk for early microvascular complications and poor long-term outcomes. Despite the availability of advanced technologies like continuous glucose monitors (CGM, e.g., Abbott Freestyle Libre) and second-generation basal insulin analogs (e.g., Sanofi's Glargine 300 U/mL, Lilly's insulin products), this gap persists, indicating that technological access alone is insufficient. A comprehensive, granular understanding of the specific, modifiable barriers in this critical transition period is urgently needed to design effective interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that poor glycemic control in young adults with T1D is primarily driven by a cluster of interacting psychosocial factors (e.g., diabetes distress, fear of hypoglycemia), life-stage-specific behavioral patterns (e.g., irregular schedules, alcohol use), and suboptimal engagement with available treatment technologies, rather than by a lack of access to advanced therapies.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 12-month prospective observational cohort study of 200 young adults (18-25) with T1D. Data will include: 1) CGM-derived metrics (glucose variability, time-in-range from Abbott Libre 3 or Dexcom G6), 2) validated psychosocial questionnaires (Diabetes Distress Scale, Hypoglycemia Fear Survey), 3) behavioral logs via a custom mobile app, and 4) linked pharmacy/insurance claims for treatment patterns. We will employ machine learning (random forest and gradient boosting models) to identify the strongest predictive features of HbA1c and time-in-range. This will be complemented by in-depth qualitative interviews (n=30) to contextualize quantitative findings.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a data-driven, hierarchical model of the most impactful barriers to glycemic control in this vulnerable population. It will move beyond generic associations to identify specific, actionable targets for intervention, such as particular facets of diabetes distress or patterns of technology disengagement. The findings will directly inform the design of tailored, age-appropriate support programs and clinical guidelines.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has described general challenges, this study uniquely integrates high-frequency CGM data, detailed psychosocial measures, and real-world treatment patterns using modern predictive analytics to deconstruct the 'poor control' phenotype into its constituent, modifiable drivers, offering a precision medicine approach to a persistent public health problem.

proposals.title: Association of Age and HbA1c with Macrovascular Complications and All-Cause Mortality in Type 2 Diabetes: A Post-Hoc Analysis of Cardiovascular Outcome Trials
proposals.motivation: Despite the widespread use of cardiovascular outcome trials (CVOTs) to establish the safety and efficacy of new glucose-lowering agents, a granular understanding of how traditional risk factors like age and glycemic control (HbA1c) interact to influence hard macrovascular endpoints and mortality remains limited. This is a critical knowledge gap, as personalized risk stratification is essential for optimizing treatment intensity and resource allocation in a heterogeneous patient population. Extending analyses beyond glycemic or renal outcomes to include major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE) and death addresses a core clinical concern in diabetes management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the risk of macrovascular complications and all-cause mortality in individuals with type 2 diabetes enrolled in CVOTs demonstrates a non-linear, age-dependent relationship with baseline HbA1c, with younger patients exhibiting a steeper risk gradient at higher HbA1c levels compared to older adults.
proposals.method: We will conduct a patient-level, post-hoc meta-analysis of publicly available data from major CVOTs for drugs like SGLT2 inhibitors and GLP-1 receptor agonists (e.g., DECLARE-TIMI 58, REWIND). The primary outcome is a composite of 3-point MACE (CV death, MI, stroke) and all-cause mortality. Using Cox proportional hazards models, we will assess the interaction between baseline age (categorized as <65, 65-75, >75 years) and HbA1c (categorized as <7.0%, 7.0-8.0%, >8.0%) on the primary outcome. We will adjust for standard CV risk factors, diabetes duration, and prior CV disease. Sensitivity analyses will explore continuous non-linear relationships using restricted cubic splines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, stratified risk profile linking fundamental clinical variables to hard endpoints, directly informing guidelines on glycemic targets tailored to age. It will clarify whether the prognostic significance of HbA1c diminishes in older adults with established CV disease, potentially supporting less stringent glycemic goals in this group. The findings will offer a benchmark for evaluating the relative cardiovascular benefit of novel therapies across different patient strata.
proposals.novelty: While CVOTs report overall treatment effects, this analysis uniquely focuses on dissecting the prognostic interplay of age and HbA1c within the trial cohorts themselves, moving beyond composite treatment effects to foundational risk stratification. It applies a detailed, patient-centered analytical framework common in epidemiology (like that seen in DPPOS analyses) to the interventional CVOT dataset, which is not routinely done.

proposals.title: Continuous Glucose Monitoring as an Effect Modifier of the HbA1c-Microvascular Complication Relationship: A Post-Hoc Analysis of the DCCT/EDIC and Other Long-Term Cohorts
proposals.motivation: While CGM and advanced insulin delivery are proven to improve glycemic control (HbA1c) and reduce hypoglycemia, their specific role in modifying the fundamental, well-established risk relationship between chronic hyperglycemia and complications remains unquantified. This is a critical knowledge gap, as it is unclear whether the same HbA1c level confers a different long-term risk for retinopathy or nephropathy in patients using modern diabetes technology versus those on conventional therapy, analogous to how a vaccine modifies the pathogen-disease relationship.
proposals.hypothesis: The use of CGM (and, secondarily, sensor-augmented pump therapy) acts as a significant negative effect modifier, attenuating the hazard ratio for microvascular complications associated with a given level of HbA1c, independent of its effect on lowering the mean HbA1c itself.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort analysis using long-term follow-up data from studies like DCCT/EDIC, where CGM use has been adopted in later phases, and from contemporary real-world datasets (e.g., T1D Exchange, linked electronic health records). The primary analysis will use Cox proportional hazards models with an interaction term between time-updated mean HbA1c (continuous) and time-updated CGM use status (yes/no). The significance of the interaction term will be tested via likelihood ratio tests. Secondary analyses will involve stratified models by technology use and meta-analytic techniques to pool hazard ratios from different cohorts.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first formal, quantitative estimate of how diabetes technology modifies the biological risk gradient of hyperglycemia, moving beyond simple comparisons of mean outcomes. It will offer a more nuanced evidence base for the value proposition of CGM, informing cost-effectiveness models and clinical guidelines by clarifying if 'lower HbA1c' and 'HbA1c with technology' represent distinct risk states.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in applying an effect modification framework—common in epidemiology (e.g., vaccine studies)—to diabetes technology evaluation, shifting the research question from 'does it lower HbA1c?' to 'does it change what HbA1c means for risk?'. This is distinct from prior CVOT or glycemic variability studies focused on direct treatment outcomes.

proposals.title: Heterogeneous Causal Effects of Glycemic Control on Diabetes Complications: A Multi-Design Causal Machine Learning Analysis
proposals.motivation: While landmark trials like ACCORD established average risks of intensive glycemic control, significant heterogeneity in treatment effect (HTE) is clinically suspected but poorly quantified. The proliferation of continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) data from devices like Abbott's FreeStyle Libre and new insulin analogs from Sanofi and Lilly provides an unprecedented opportunity to move beyond HbA1c averages and model complex glycemic exposure. A pre-2026 evidence gap exists in causally estimating how the effect of glycemic control on cardiovascular and renal outcomes varies across individual patient profiles.
proposals.hypothesis: The causal effect of improved glycemic control (via HbA1c reduction) on major diabetes complications is heterogeneous and can be predicted by a patient's clinical profile, including diabetes duration, age, comorbidity burden, and specific treatment regimens (e.g., GLP-1 RA or SGLT2i use).
proposals.method: We will apply causal machine learning methods, specifically meta-learners like the R-learner and X-learner, to estimate conditional average treatment effects (CATE). Treatment (glycemic control) will be instrumented using quasi-experimental designs: 1) an instrumental variable (IV) approach leveraging regional variation in formulary access to second-generation basal insulins (e.g., Gla-300, Degludec), 2) a difference-in-differences (DiD) analysis around the initiation of CGM use, and 3) a propensity-score-based approach. These designs will provide multiple robust inputs for HTE estimation. Models will be trained on linked EHR and claims data, with CGM-derived metrics (e.g., glucose variability, time-in-range) as secondary exposures.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, personalized risk-benefit profile for intensive glycemic management, identifying subgroups that derive disproportionate benefit or risk. It will provide a methodological framework for integrating multiple causal designs to strengthen HTE inference. The results will offer a more nuanced, data-driven foundation for clinical guidelines and shared decision-making in type 2 diabetes management.
proposals.novelty: This integrates multiple quasi-experimental identification strategies within a single causal ML framework for HTE estimation in diabetes, moving beyond single-design average effects. It explicitly leverages contemporary treatment and monitoring tools (next-gen insulins, CGM) as instruments and effect modifiers, a synthesis not yet common in pre-2026 causal epidemiology.

proposals.title: Causal Inference of Glycemic Exposure: A Mendelian Randomization Study of HbA1c-Associated Genetic Variants on Microvascular and Cardiovascular Complications in the UK Biobank
proposals.motivation: While intensive glycemic control reduces complications in clinical trials, the specific causal effect of HbA1c across the physiological range in real-world populations remains obscured by confounding from lifestyle, treatment adherence, and reverse causality in observational studies. This limits precise risk stratification and the evaluation of novel glucose-lowering agents from companies like Sanofi and Lilly. Establishing a causal link is critical for validating HbA1c as a primary target for intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that genetic variants known to influence HbA1c levels (e.g., in GCK, G6PC2, HK1) act as instrumental variables and will demonstrate a causal, dose-response relationship with the risk of diabetic complications, independent of traditional confounding factors.
proposals.method: We will perform a two-sample Mendelian Randomization (MR) analysis using pre-2025 GWAS summary statistics for HbA1c to select strong, independent genetic instruments. These instruments will be applied to outcome data from the UK Biobank, defining complications (retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy, CVD) via linked hospital records and defined algorithms. Primary analysis will use inverse-variance weighted MR, with sensitivity analyses (MR-Egger, weighted median) to assess pleiotropy. We will also conduct stratified analyses by diabetes status and medication use.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, genetically informed causal estimate of HbA1c on a spectrum of complications, strengthening the evidence base for glycemic targets. It will generate novel insights into whether the relationship is consistent across complication types and in populations with and without diagnosed diabetes, informing primary and secondary prevention strategies.
proposals.novelty: While MR has been applied to cardiometabolic traits, its focused application to dissect the causal effect of HbA1c specifically on diabetes complications—moving beyond association to causation in a contemporary cohort—is novel. It directly addresses limitations highlighted in recent CVOT and CGM trials regarding confounding in outcome studies.

proposals.title: Causal Inference for Personalized Glycemic Management: Estimating the Net Benefit of Delayed HbA1c Titration Using Real-World Data
proposals.motivation: Despite evidence from CVOTs and trials like InRange showing the importance of glycemic variability and hypoglycemia, clinical inertia in diabetes is universally framed as a failure. However, rapid intensification to lower HbA1c can increase treatment burden, hypoglycemia risk, and worsen quality of life. A data-driven framework is needed to determine when a delay in achieving glycemic targets is a rational, patient-centered trade-off rather than suboptimal care.
proposals.hypothesis: For specific patient phenotypes (e.g., defined by age, hypoglycemia history, or comorbidity burden), a deliberate delay in HbA1c reduction following treatment initiation or intensification is causally associated with a superior net benefit on a composite endpoint of clinical safety and patient-reported outcomes, compared to standard timely titration.
proposals.method: We will use a regression discontinuity design (RDD), leveraging clinical guideline-recommended HbA1c thresholds (e.g., 7.0%, 8.0%) as instruments for treatment intensification timing. Data from real-world sources (e.g., Abbott Libre CGM-linked claims, Lilly/Sanofi insulin registries) will be analyzed. The primary outcome is a personalized composite endpoint weighting time-in-range, severe hypoglycemia events, treatment regimen complexity, and quality-of-life scores. Causal forest algorithms will be used for heterogeneous treatment effect estimation to identify beneficiary phenotypes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first causal, quantitative framework for evaluating the net benefit of titration timing, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all view of clinical inertia. It will generate a phenotype-specific evidence base to guide shared decision-making, potentially justifying deliberate delays as optimal personalized strategy in specific subgroups.
proposals.novelty: It reframes 'delay' from a quality metric failure to a testable therapeutic hypothesis using causal econometric methods not typically applied to titration timing. It integrates hard outcomes from CVOTs with patient-centered metrics into a single causal estimand for net benefit.

proposals.title: Time-to-Event Modeling of Microvascular Complications in Type 2 Diabetes: A Retrospective Cohort Study Using Real-World EHR Data to Predict Progression from Elevated HbA1c
proposals.motivation: While cardiovascular outcome trials (CVOTs) have transformed the management of macrovascular risk in diabetes, predicting the timing of microvascular complications (retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy) remains a critical unmet need. Real-world evidence (RWE) from electronic health records (EHR) offers a rich, longitudinal data source to model disease progression, complementing insights from randomized controlled trials like InRange which focus on short-term glycemic variability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a survival analysis model incorporating time-updated HbA1c trajectories, treatment patterns (e.g., use of second-generation basal insulins like glargine 300 U/mL or degludec, GLP-1 RAs), and baseline risk factors can accurately predict the time-to-first microvascular complication following an index period of elevated HbA1c (≥7.5%).
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using a large, de-identified EHR database (e.g., Optum, TriNetX). The primary outcome is a composite time-to-event of first documented diagnosis of diabetic retinopathy, nephropathy, or neuropathy. We will employ Cox proportional hazards models with time-varying covariates for HbA1c and medication exposures (e.g., insulin analogs from Sanofi/Lilly, GLP-1 RAs). Model performance will be assessed using Harrell's C-index and calibration plots, with sensitivity analyses for competing risks (e.g., cardiovascular events).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a validated, real-world risk prediction tool for microvascular complication timing, directly informing personalized monitoring schedules and treatment intensification strategies. It will extend the clinical trial-derived evidence base (e.g., from DPPOS and CVOTs) by quantifying progression risks in routine care settings, where patient adherence and treatment patterns are more heterogeneous.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior risk scores focused on static prediction or cardiovascular outcomes, this work applies rigorous time-to-event methodology from clinical trials directly to dynamic EHR data to model the *timing* of microvascular complications, integrating modern treatment exposures as time-varying predictors.

proposals.title: Causal Drivers of Glycemic Outcomes in Pediatric Type 1 Diabetes: A Hybrid Registry-Intervention Platform Using Developmentally-Tailored Data and Targeted Learning
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in insulin analogs (e.g., Sanofi's Glargine 300 U/mL, Lilly's formulations) and CGM technology (e.g., Abbott systems), glycemic outcomes in children and adolescents with T1D remain highly variable, with significant risk for acute complications. Current real-world evidence (RWE) studies and CVOTs primarily focus on adult populations, lacking a developmentally-sensitive framework to identify modifiable factors across childhood. A platform integrating passive device data with active psychosocial assessments is needed to move beyond correlation to actionable, causal insights for personalized pediatric care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the causal effects of specific, modifiable factors—such as daily routines, caregiver involvement patterns, and psychosocial stressors—on glycemic variability and hypoglycemia risk are non-stationary and vary significantly across developmental stages (e.g., early childhood, adolescence).
proposals.method: We will establish a longitudinal cohort of 500 youth with T1D, aged 2-17, collecting continuous passive data from CGMs and connected insulin delivery devices. This will be paired with periodic, developmentally-tailored active assessments via ecological momentary assessment (EMA) for youth and caregivers. We will employ advanced causal inference methodologies, specifically Targeted Maximum Likelihood Estimation (TMLE) and longitudinal g-methods, to estimate the stage-specific effects of time-varying exposures (e.g., sleep duration, meal timing, stress) on CGM-derived outcomes (Time-in-Range, glucose variability), rigorously adjusting for time-dependent confounding.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, sustainable data platform for pediatric diabetes RWE, co-designed for ethical engagement. It will generate the first causal maps of developmentally-specific drivers of glycemic outcomes, providing immediately testable hypotheses for personalized behavioral and clinical interventions. The platform will serve as a foundation for future pragmatic trials of precision medicine strategies in this population.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior observational studies (e.g., InRange trial) that assess device performance, this platform uniquely integrates developmental science with state-of-the-art causal inference (TMLE) on high-frequency, hybrid data to identify dynamic, actionable treatment targets rather than static correlations.

proposals.title: Modeling the Bidirectional Causal Pathways Between Glycemic Control and Early Microvascular Complications in Type 2 Diabetes: A Longitudinal Causal Inference Study
proposals.motivation: While the causal link from hyperglycemia to complications is well-established, clinical observation suggests a potential feedback loop where early complications may themselves worsen glycemic control, creating a vicious cycle. This bidirectional relationship is poorly quantified, limiting our ability to design interventions that break this cycle. Recent data from cardiovascular and kidney outcomes trials (CVOTs) and continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) studies provide the granular, longitudinal data needed to investigate this.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a bidirectional causal loop exists: elevated glucose variability and HbA1c increase the risk of early microvascular complications, and the onset of these complications subsequently degrades subsequent glycemic control, mediated by factors like changes in self-management behavior and physiological stress.
proposals.method: We will apply a longitudinal causal inference framework, specifically a cross-lagged panel model (CLPM) with structured nested mean models (SNMMs), to deconfound time-varying factors. Using data from sources like the DPPOS and linked CGM (e.g., Abbott Freestyle Libre) and electronic health record data, we will model time-lagged effects between glycemic parameters (HbA1c, CGM-derived glucose variability) and early complication indicators (e.g., albuminuria, retinal changes). Mediation analysis will test behavioral (self-care metrics) and physiological (inflammatory markers) pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated structural equation model quantifying the bidirectional causal pathways, identifying critical time windows and patient subgroups where the feedback loop is most active. It will offer a robust, causal evidence base for designing timely, targeted interventions—potentially using newer agents from Sanofi or Lilly—to prevent cycle escalation.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond predictive risk modeling to formal causal inference on the feedback hypothesis, and leveraging contemporary CGM and complication data to model dynamic, time-lagged relationships that previous epidemiological studies could not capture.

proposals.title: Modeling the Developmental Trajectory of Glycemic Control: A Machine Learning Approach to Predict HbA1c Deterioration from Early Complications in Pediatric Type 1 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: In pediatric type 1 diabetes (T1D), the transition through adolescence is a high-risk period for deterioration in glycemic control, increasing long-term complication risk. Current models, like those from CVOTs, focus on adult outcomes and lack granularity for developmental stages. There is a critical need to identify children at risk for control loss before it manifests in elevated HbA1c, enabling preemptive, developmentally tailored interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: Early, subclinical signs of diabetes-related complications (e.g., hypoglycemia unawareness, microalbuminuria, psychosocial stress) and their interaction with maturational variables (pubertal stage, growth velocity) contain predictive signals for future glycemic control deterioration, and these signals can be modeled to create dynamic, age-specific risk scores.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal analysis of existing pediatric T1D cohort data (e.g., from DPPOS-style studies or registries) with serial CGM (e.g., Abbott FreeStyle Libre) and clinical data. Using time-to-event (survival) analysis and recurrent neural networks (RNNs), we will model the temporal relationship between early complication markers and a primary outcome of clinically significant HbA1c increase. Key covariates will include puberty (Tanner stage), insulin regimen (e.g., Lilly or Sanofi analogs), and psychosocial metrics. Model performance will be validated against held-out data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, dynamic risk model for glycemic deterioration specific to pediatric development, moving beyond static adult-focused risk scores. It will identify actionable, pre-clinical predictors, directly informing the timing and target of interventions. The findings will contribute to a developmental framework for understanding T1D progression.
proposals.novelty: This study inverts the traditional complication paradigm by asking if early complications predict control loss, not vice-versa, and explicitly models how this relationship is modulated by key maturational processes, a perspective not addressed in prior outcomes research like the InRange trial or CVOT summaries.

proposals.title: Early Microvascular Complications as Predictors of Subsequent Glycemic Control Deterioration: A Longitudinal Analysis of the DPPOS Cohort
proposals.motivation: The causal direction from glycemic control to complications is well-established. However, a bidirectional relationship is plausible, where early complications may signal or drive subsequent metabolic dysregulation, creating a vicious cycle. This inverse problem is understudied but critical for understanding disease progression and personalizing treatment strategies, especially given the focus on cardiorenal outcomes (CVOTs) and advanced insulin analogs (e.g., glargine 300 U/mL, degludec).
proposals.hypothesis: Early, subclinical signs of microvascular complications (e.g., retinal changes, microalbuminuria, or peripheral neuropathy) predict a subsequent accelerated loss of glycemic control (increased HbA1c and glucose variability) in individuals with type 2 diabetes, independent of baseline treatment and traditional risk factors.
proposals.method: We will analyze longitudinal data from the Diabetes Prevention Program Outcomes Study (DPPOS), leveraging its detailed complication assessments and glycemic measures. Using time-dependent Cox proportional hazards models and mixed-effects models, we will test if incident complications predict future HbA1c trajectories. We will incorporate continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) metrics (e.g., coefficient of variation, time-in-range) from a subset, inspired by InRange trial methods, to assess glucose variability as a secondary outcome. Machine learning techniques (e.g., random survival forests) will be used to identify key complication signatures most predictive of control loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide novel evidence for a bidirectional complication-control model, challenging the unidirectional paradigm. It will identify high-risk patients for whom early complication detection should trigger intensified monitoring and possibly earlier intervention with newer therapies (e.g., GLP-1 RAs, SGLT2 inhibitors, or second-generation basal insulins) to break the cycle.
proposals.novelty: While most research examines control predicting complications, we explicitly test the inverse temporal relationship. We integrate complication data from DPPOS with modern CGM-derived glycemic variability metrics, moving beyond HbA1c alone to a more nuanced view of control.

proposals.title: Characterizing the Protective Phenotype: A Multi-Domain Analysis of Factors Associated with Sustained Glycemic Control (HbA1c <7%) Without Severe Hypoglycemia in Type 1 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Achieving HbA1c <7% without severe hypoglycemia (SH) is the central therapeutic challenge in type 1 diabetes (T1D) management, as highlighted by outcomes from trials of second-generation basal insulins (e.g., InRange trial) and CVOTs. While risk factors for SH are well-studied, the specific behavioral, technological, physiological, and pharmacological factors that *protect* individuals within this optimal control subgroup remain poorly defined. Identifying these protective factors is critical for developing targeted interventions to help more patients achieve this dual goal.
proposals.hypothesis: Individuals with T1D who maintain HbA1c <7% without SH represent a distinct phenotype characterized by a specific, modifiable combination of factors, including lower glucose variability, specific patterns of continuous glucose monitor (CGM) use, adherence to certain treatment regimens (e.g., use of second-generation analogs like glargine 300 U/mL or degludec), and structured self-management behaviors.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of existing RCT and observational cohort data (e.g., from trials like InRange, data from Abbott CGM real-world evidence programs). The target population will be adults with T1D. We will use propensity score matching to create a comparator group (HbA1c ≥7% and/or with SH). Machine learning algorithms, specifically random forest and gradient boosting models, will be employed to identify the most predictive features of the optimal outcome from a multi-domain dataset including CGM metrics (e.g., %TIR, CV, LBGI), insulin regimen details, demographic data, and patient-reported behaviors. Logistic regression will then quantify the adjusted odds ratios for key protective factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond identifying risk to explicitly model the 'protective phenotype' for optimal T1D outcomes. It will provide a data-driven profile of the factors most strongly associated with success, offering clinicians a practical framework for personalized treatment intensification. The findings will inform the design of future interventions aimed at replicating this protective profile in a broader patient population.
proposals.novelty: While prior research often treats low HbA1c and hypoglycemia as separate endpoints, this study explicitly focuses on their concurrent achievement as a composite optimal outcome. The application of machine learning to multi-source data to define a *protective* profile, rather than a risk profile, represents a novel, patient-centric approach to outcomes research in diabetes.

proposals.title: Dynamic Adherence Pathways to Cardiovascular Risk: A Longitudinal Causal Mediation Study of Insulin Titration Behavior in Type 2 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Despite proven efficacy of modern basal insulin analogs (e.g., Sanofi's Glargine 300 U/mL, Lilly's Degludec), suboptimal titration remains a major barrier to glycemic control, contributing to long-term complications. While CVOTs (e.g., LEADER, REWIND) have established cardiorenal benefits of some glucose-lowering agents, the causal pathway linking real-world titration adherence dynamics, glycemic variability (as measured by Abbott's FreeStyle Libre CGM), and incident macrovascular events remains poorly characterized, limiting targeted behavioral interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: That dynamic, time-varying patterns of insulin titration adherence, measured repeatedly, mediate a significant portion of the effect of baseline healthcare provider and patient attitudes on long-term macrovascular risk, and that this mediation is moderated by age and baseline HbA1c.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, prospective cohort study of adults with T2D initiating or intensifying basal insulin therapy. Attitudes, self-reported and electronically-monitored titration behavior (via connected pens/apps), and glycemic outcomes (HbA1c, CGM-derived metrics) will be measured at 0, 3, 6, 12 months and then annually. Extended follow-up (≥5 years) will capture hard endpoints (MACE, mortality). We will employ longitudinal structural equation modeling (specifically, latent growth curve models within a structural equation framework) to model adherence trajectories and test them as time-varying mediators in the causal pathway to macrovascular outcomes, adjusting for confounders.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond cross-sectional correlations to identify specific, dynamic behavioral patterns that causally influence cardiovascular risk. It will provide a quantitative model to pinpoint high-leverage intervention moments in the titration process, directly informing the design of timely, personalized adherence support tools to improve long-term outcomes.
proposals.novelty: This integrates real-world digital adherence data, advanced longitudinal causal mediation analysis, and extended hard endpoint follow-up—a methodological synergy not yet applied to the insulin titration problem—to shift from describing non-adherence to predicting its downstream clinical consequences.

proposals.title: Mapping the Healthcare Ecosystem: A Cross-Cultural Analysis of Systemic Determinants of Insulin Titration Attitudes and Behaviors
proposals.motivation: Despite the proven efficacy of insulin titration for glycemic control, clinical inertia and patient reluctance remain significant barriers, contributing to poor outcomes. Current research, including CVOTs and trials like InRange, focuses on drug-specific outcomes but inadequately addresses how the broader healthcare system environment influences the human factors of titration. Understanding the systemic drivers of attitudes is crucial for designing effective, scalable implementation strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific, quantifiable features of a national healthcare system (e.g., reimbursement models, clinical decision support infrastructure) are the primary predictors of patient and provider attitudes towards insulin titration, independent of individual demographic or clinical factors.
proposals.method: We will adapt a cultural ecology framework to define and operationalize key healthcare ecosystem variables (e.g., payment structure rigidity, HbA1c target enforcement, CGM/telemedicine penetration) for 6-8 diverse countries (e.g., US, UK, Germany, Japan, India). A standardized, cross-culturally validated survey will be administered to cohorts of insulin-treated patients with T2D and their treating physicians in each country, assessing attitudes (self-efficacy, fear of hypoglycemia, perceived complexity). We will employ multivariate hierarchical regression modeling to test the association between systemic-level variables and attitude scores, controlling for individual-level covariates.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond descriptive international comparisons to provide a mechanistic, evidence-based model linking healthcare system design to titration behavior. It will identify actionable systemic levers for intervention, offering policymakers and health technology developers (e.g., Abbott, Lilly, Sanofi) targeted strategies to reduce clinical inertia and improve real-world glycemic outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While prior work compares clinical outcomes or describes attitudes in single systems, this is the first to formally adapt an ecological systems theory framework to quantitatively test the causal influence of macro-level healthcare structures on the micro-psychology of diabetes management.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial Investigating the Differential Effects of Sucralose, Stevia, and Saccharin on Gut Microbiota Composition, Glucose Variability, and Cardiometabolic Risk in Individuals with Prediabetes or Early Type 2 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Low- and no-calorie sweeteners (LNCS) are widely consumed by individuals with diabetes for glycemic control, yet pre-2026 evidence suggests they may alter gut microbiota in ways that could paradoxically impair glucose metabolism and increase cardiometabolic risk. This is a critical knowledge gap, as existing studies are small, short-term, and often in healthy populations, not in the at-risk groups who consume them most. Understanding the specific, comparative impact of common LNCS on the diabetic gut microbiome is essential for evidence-based dietary guidance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chronic consumption of specific LNCS (sucralose, stevia, saccharin) will induce distinct, quantifiable shifts in gut microbiota composition and function in individuals with prediabetes or type 2 diabetes, and that these microbial changes will be associated with worsened continuous glucose monitoring (CGM)-derived glycemic variability and unfavorable changes in established cardiovascular outcome trial (CVOT) risk markers, such as high-sensitivity C-reactive protein.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 12-week, double-blind, randomized, controlled parallel-group trial in 150 adults with prediabetes or diet-controlled early T2D. Participants will be randomized to one of five arms: daily supplementation with sucralose, stevia glycosides, saccharin, glucose (caloric control), or water (non-caloric control). The primary outcome will be change in gut microbiota alpha- and beta-diversity (16S rRNA sequencing, analyzed via QIIME2). Secondary outcomes include CGM metrics (e.g., coefficient of variation, time-in-range from Abbott Freestyle Libre Pro), oral glucose tolerance, and serum cardiometabolic risk markers. Statistical analysis will employ linear mixed-effects models to assess group-by-time interactions, with mediation analysis to test if microbiota changes explain metabolic outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first head-to-head comparison of the long-term effects of major LNCS on the gut microbiome in a clinically relevant diabetic population. It will directly link microbial shifts to gold-standard glycemic variability metrics and emerging CVOT-relevant risk pathways, moving beyond association to mechanistic insight. The findings will generate crucial data for patients, clinicians, and public health authorities to weigh the potential risks and benefits of LNCS use in diabetes management.
proposals.novelty: While prior research has shown LNCS can affect microbiota in rodents or healthy humans, this study's novelty lies in its targeted focus on a prediabetic/T2D cohort, its simultaneous evaluation of three structurally distinct sweeteners against both caloric and non-caloric controls, and its integrated analysis linking specific microbial signatures to continuous glucose profiles and pre-specified cardiometabolic risk indices.

proposals.title: Differential Effects of Low- and No-Calorie Sweeteners on Glycemic Control and Insulin Resistance Phenotypes: A Randomized Controlled Trial Using Continuous Glucose Monitoring
proposals.motivation: Low- and no-calorie sweeteners (LNCS) are widely recommended for diabetes management, yet population-level studies show conflicting effects on glycemic control and metabolic health. This heterogeneity may mask significant effects in specific patient subgroups. With the rise of continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) and the ability to define insulin resistance phenotypes more precisely, a targeted investigation is needed to guide personalized dietary recommendations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the metabolic effects of LNCS consumption, measured by CGM-derived metrics of glucose variability and time-in-range, will differ significantly between individuals with predominant hepatic versus peripheral/muscle insulin resistance phenotypes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized, double-blind, crossover trial in adults with type 2 diabetes or prediabetes. Participants will be stratified into hepatic or peripheral insulin resistance phenotypes using the oral glucose insulin sensitivity (OGIS) index and the Matsuda index derived from a 75g oral glucose tolerance test. Using Abbott Freestyle Libre Pro CGM, participants will undergo three dietary intervention phases: LNCS (sucralose/acesulfame-K), non-nutritive sweetener (stevia/monk fruit), and a sucrose control. The primary outcome is the difference in mean amplitude of glycemic excursions (MAGE) between interventions within each phenotype. Secondary outcomes include time-in-range (70-180 mg/dL) and postprandial glucose iAUC.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first phenotype-specific evidence on LNCS effects on real-world glycemic control, moving beyond population averages. It will inform the development of personalized nutrition guidelines for diabetes management and offer a mechanistic framework for understanding inter-individual variability in response to food additives.
proposals.novelty: While prior LNCS research has focused on broad populations or weight outcomes, this study uniquely applies precise metabolic phenotyping, a cornerstone of modern diabetes research (e.g., InRange trial methodology), to a dietary intervention, leveraging CGM to capture nuanced glycemic effects.

proposals.title: Dissociation of Albuminuria Reduction from Glycemic Control in SGLT2 Inhibitor Therapy: A Post-Hoc Analysis of CVOT and Real-World Data
proposals.motivation: SGLT2 inhibitors (SGLT2i) confer significant renal and cardiovascular benefits in patients with type 2 diabetes (T2D), as established in pre-2026 cardiovascular outcomes trials (CVOTs). However, the extent to which these renoprotective effects, specifically reductions in urinary albumin-to-creatinine ratio (UACR), are directly dependent on concurrent improvements in glycemic control (HbA1c) remains ambiguous. Clarifying this relationship is crucial for understanding the drug's mechanism and for optimizing treatment strategies in patients with chronic kidney disease (CKD) where glycemic targets may already be met or where HbA1c response is attenuated.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the reduction in UACR observed with SGLT2i therapy is largely independent of the magnitude of HbA1c reduction, suggesting that primary renoprotective mechanisms extend beyond glucose-lowering.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pooled, patient-level, post-hoc analysis of major SGLT2i CVOTs (e.g., DECLARE-TIMI 58, EMPA-REG OUTCOME, CANVAS) and real-world data from sources like the Abbott Freestyle Libre CGM registry. The primary analysis will use multivariable linear mixed-effects models to assess the relationship between the change in UACR (log-transformed) from baseline to 6 and 12 months and the concurrent change in HbA1c, adjusting for baseline covariates (e.g., eGFR, baseline HbA1c, cardiovascular history, concomitant treatments from Lilly, Sanofi, etc.). We will specifically test for interaction terms between treatment arm (SGLT2i vs. placebo) and ΔHbA1c on ΔUACR.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, quantitative evidence on the glycemic independence of SGLT2i's albuminuria-lowering effect, a key mediator of long-term renal outcomes. It will inform clinical guidelines by supporting the use of SGLT2i for renoprotection even in patients with well-controlled diabetes or advanced CKD, where glycemic efficacy may be limited. The findings will also stimulate further research into non-glycemic pathways, such as tubuloglomerular feedback and metabolic effects.
proposals.novelty: While CVOTs have established renal benefit, no pre-2026 analysis has systematically quantified the dissociation between UACR and HbA1c trajectories using pooled trial and high-frequency CGM data to rigorously test the independence hypothesis, moving beyond simple correlation.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of a GLP-1 Receptor Agonist for the Preservation of Renal Function in Type 1 Diabetes with Persistent Albuminuria: The T1D-PRO Study
proposals.motivation: Cardiovascular and renal outcome trials (CVOTs) have revolutionized the management of type 2 diabetes (T2D), establishing clear benefits for SGLT2 inhibitors and GLP-1 receptor agonists. However, these landmark studies have largely excluded patients with type 1 diabetes (T1D), creating a critical evidence gap. T1D patients with albuminuria represent a high-risk population for progressive chronic kidney disease (CKD) and end-stage renal disease, yet they lack dedicated, prospective renal outcome data for newer therapeutic classes beyond renin-angiotensin system blockade.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adjunctive therapy with a GLP-1 receptor agonist (specifically, the once-weekly agent dulaglutide from Lilly), compared to placebo, will significantly reduce the rate of estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) decline in T1D patients with established albuminuria who are on optimized insulin therapy.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-center, double-blind, placebo-controlled, event-driven trial. Approximately 1,200 participants with T1D (C-peptide negative), persistent albuminuria (UACR 30-5000 mg/g), and eGFR ≥25 mL/min/1.73m² will be randomized 1:1 to dulaglutide 1.5 mg or placebo, added to standard care including ACEi/ARB. The primary outcome is the time to a composite of sustained ≥40% eGFR decline, progression to end-stage kidney disease, or renal death. Key secondary outcomes include slope of eGFR decline, changes in UACR, and cardiovascular events. Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) data (using Abbott Freestyle Libre or Dexcom systems) will be collected to rigorously assess glycemic control and variability as exploratory mediators.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence for the renal efficacy and safety of a non-insulin adjunctive therapy specifically in T1D with CKD. It will directly inform clinical guidelines for this underserved population. Furthermore, by integrating CGM metrics, it will elucidate the relationship between glucose control variability and renal outcomes in the context of a novel intervention.
proposals.novelty: While CVOTs like REWIND (dulaglutide) showed renal benefit in T2D, no dedicated renal outcome trial has been conducted in T1D with albuminuria. This proposal shifts the paradigm from extrapolating T2D data to generating direct evidence for a high-risk T1D phenotype, a population explicitly excluded from prior major trials.

proposals.title: Patient-Reported Outcomes as Primary Endpoints in Adjunctive Therapy Trials for Type 2 Diabetes: A Randomized Controlled Trial of GLP-1/GIP Receptor Agonists
proposals.motivation: Despite the proven cardiovascular and renal benefits of newer adjunctive therapies (e.g., GLP-1/GIP receptor agonists like tirzepatide from Lilly), clinical trials remain anchored to HbA1c as the primary endpoint. This fails to capture patient-centric priorities such as treatment satisfaction and quality of life, which are critical for long-term adherence and real-world effectiveness. The 2022 CVOT Summit highlighted the need to integrate patient-reported outcomes (PROs) more meaningfully into the evaluation of diabetes treatments.
proposals.hypothesis: In individuals with type 2 diabetes on stable basal insulin (e.g., Sanofi's Glargine 300 U/mL or Novo Nordisk's Degludec), prioritizing patient-reported outcomes (PROs) like treatment satisfaction and weight-related quality of life as primary endpoints in a trial of adjunctive GLP-1/GIP therapy will demonstrate non-inferior glycemic control (HbA1c) while achieving superior patient-centered outcomes compared to a trial design prioritizing HbA1c reduction.
proposals.method: We propose a 12-month, multi-center, open-label, randomized controlled trial. Participants (n=450) with T2D on basal insulin will be randomized 1:1 to one of two endpoint hierarchy arms: (1) PRO-Primary (primary: change in Diabetes Treatment Satisfaction Questionnaire status version [DTSQs] and Impact of Weight on Quality of Life-Lite [IWQOL-Lite]; secondary: HbA1c, CGM metrics), or (2) HbA1c-Primary (primary: change in HbA1c; secondary: PROs). Both arms receive the same adjunctive therapy (tirzepatide). Glycemic control will be assessed via Abbott's FreeStyle Libre CGM for glucose variability metrics. Statistical analysis will employ a non-inferiority test for HbA1c between arms and superiority tests for PROs.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel framework for designing diabetes trials that formally elevates patient experience to a co-equal or primary status alongside traditional biomarkers. It will generate evidence on whether this paradigm shift impacts glycemic outcomes, potentially informing future regulatory and clinical guidelines. The results could redefine success in diabetes management, aligning clinical research more closely with patient values and long-term adherence drivers.
proposals.novelty: While trials like InRange use CGM to compare insulin analogs, and DPPOS examines lifestyle scores, no study has prospectively randomized the endpoint hierarchy itself to test whether prioritizing PROs alters the perceived or actual value of a pharmacological intervention. This directly challenges the entrenched glyco-centric trial design model.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Real-World, CGM-Informed Protocol for Diabetic Ketoacidosis Risk Mitigation During SGLT2 Inhibitor Therapy in Type 1 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: SGLT2 inhibitors offer glycemic and cardiorenal benefits in type 1 diabetes (T1D), but their use is limited by the risk of euglycemic diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA). Current risk mitigation guidelines are generic and lack specific, actionable thresholds for insulin adjustment and ketone monitoring based on real-time glucose data. This gap hinders safe, widespread adoption of a potentially beneficial adjunctive therapy.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured protocol, integrating continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) metrics (e.g., time-in-range, glucose trends) with specific insulin dose adjustment rules and tiered ketone monitoring triggers, will significantly reduce the incidence of DKA and DKA-risk events compared to standard care in real-world users of SGLT2 inhibitors for T1D.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, randomized controlled trial. The intervention arm will use a novel protocol: 1) Algorithm-driven insulin reduction (e.g., 10-20% basal) at therapy initiation if CGM shows >80% time-in-range (70-180 mg/dL); 2) Real-time alerts for ketone check triggered by CGM trend analysis (e.g., stable glucose <150 mg/dL for >6 hours or rapid decline >3 mg/dL/min); 3) A stepped carbohydrate intake and insulin algorithm for 'sick-day' CGM patterns. The control arm receives standard education. The primary outcome is the rate of DKA and pre-DKA events (blood ketone >1.5 mmol/L) over 12 months, analyzed using time-to-event and Poisson regression models.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first validated, data-driven clinical protocol for SGLT2i use in T1D, moving beyond consensus statements to an actionable tool. It will define precise CGM-derived thresholds for preventive action, potentially enabling safer integration of SGLT2 inhibitors into T1D management to improve cardiovascular and renal outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., InRange trial) used CGM to assess glucose variability, this proposal innovatively uses CGM data streams as direct inputs for a predictive, intervention-triggering risk mitigation algorithm, creating a dynamic safety framework rather than a static set of recommendations.

proposals.title: A Real-World, CGM-Based Assessment of Hypoglycemia Risk and Glycemic Outcomes with Adjunctive SGLT2 Inhibitor Therapy in High-Risk Adults with Type 1 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Despite the proven cardiovascular and renal benefits of SGLT2 inhibitors in type 2 diabetes, their adjunctive use in type 1 diabetes (T1D) remains controversial and off-label, primarily due to the risk of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA). Pre-2026, real-world data on their safety and efficacy in specific, vulnerable T1D subpopulations—particularly those with high baseline hypoglycemia risk—are sparse. This gap hinders personalized treatment decisions for patients who may benefit from improved glycemic control without exacerbating hypoglycemia.
proposals.hypothesis: In adults with T1D and high baseline hypoglycemia risk, adjunctive SGLT2 inhibitor therapy will be associated with reduced glucose variability and time-in-hyperglycemia, without a significant increase in clinically significant hypoglycemia or DKA events, when compared to propensity-matched controls on insulin-only regimens.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, observational cohort study using linked electronic health records and continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) data from a large, integrated health system (e.g., data partnerships with Abbott or Dexcom). The cohort will include adults with T1D, defined by diagnosis codes and insulin dependence, who have a high hypoglycemia risk (defined as >5% time <70 mg/dL on baseline CGM). We will identify new initiators of SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) and match them 1:3 to non-initiators using propensity scores based on age, diabetes duration, HbA1c, renal function, and insulin regimen. Primary outcomes, assessed over 6 months, will be CGM-derived metrics: coefficient of variation (CV), time-in-range (70-180 mg/dL), and time-below-range (<70 mg/dL). Safety outcomes will include severe hypoglycemia and DKA events, identified via diagnostic codes and lab values. Statistical analysis will employ mixed-effects models to compare changes in CGM metrics.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide crucial, real-world evidence on the nuanced risk-benefit profile of SGLT2 inhibitors in a high-risk T1D population often excluded from clinical trials. It will directly inform clinical practice by quantifying expected changes in CGM metrics, offering a more granular view of glycemic outcomes beyond HbA1c. The findings will help clinicians identify which patients with T1D might safely benefit from adjunctive therapy.
proposals.novelty: While prior RCTs like DEPICT studied SGLT2 inhibitors in broad T1D populations, this proposal focuses specifically on the high hypoglycemia risk subgroup using high-frequency, real-world CGM data—a novel endpoint not fully captured in traditional trials. The use of propensity score matching on detailed CGM-derived baseline risk represents a methodological advance in real-world evidence generation for diabetes therapeutics.

proposals.title: A Proof-of-Concept Study on Transient SGLT Activation for Hypoglycemia Prevention in Type 1 Diabetes: A Mechanistic CGM-Based Analysis
proposals.motivation: Hypoglycemia remains a critical barrier to safe glycemic control in insulin-treated diabetes, despite advances in insulin analogs and CGM technology. SGLT2 inhibitors are known to lower glucose by increasing urinary excretion, but the reverse pharmacological action—transient SGLT activation to enhance renal glucose reabsorption—has not been explored as a potential physiological buffer against falling glucose levels. This concept addresses a fundamental unmet need in diabetes management: a rapid, endogenous mechanism to counteract hypoglycemia.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pharmacological agents capable of transiently activating renal SGLT (primarily SGLT2) transporters will increase renal glucose reabsorption, thereby attenuating the rate and severity of glucose decline during insulin-induced hypoglycemia in individuals with Type 1 Diabetes.
proposals.method: We propose a randomized, double-blind, crossover study in adults with T1D using MDI or insulin pumps. Participants will undergo two standardized insulin-induced hypoglycemic clamps. In one session, they will receive a pre-emptive intravenous bolus of a novel, fast-acting SGLT activator (a candidate molecule identified from a pre-2026 Sanofi compound library screen); the other session will use placebo. Continuous Glucose Monitoring (Abbott Freestyle Libre 2 or Dexcom G6) and frequent venous sampling will measure glucose dynamics, with a primary endpoint of the time spent below 70 mg/dL and the nadir glucose level. Pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic modeling will quantify the relationship between drug concentration and the rate of glucose decline.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first human evidence for 'renal glucose rescue' as a novel therapeutic principle for hypoglycemia prevention. It will establish key pharmacodynamic parameters for SGLT activators and could directly inform the development of a new class of adjunctive therapy for diabetes, aimed specifically at reducing hypoglycemic risk, a major outcome of concern in CVOTs.
proposals.novelty: While SGLT2 inhibition is a well-established glucose-lowering strategy, the deliberate pharmacological activation of SGLT for glycemic benefit is a radical inversion of the current paradigm. This approach is distinct from traditional counter-regulatory hormone responses and leverages the kidney as an active glucose-regulating organ in a novel, protective manner.

proposals.title: Phenotypic and Glycemic Predictors of Optimal Glycemic Control with Basal-Bolus Insulin Monotherapy in Type 2 Diabetes: A Post-Hoc Analysis of CVOT and CGM Trial Data
proposals.motivation: Despite the proliferation of adjunctive non-insulin therapies (e.g., GLP-1 RAs, SGLT2i) to improve outcomes, their use adds complexity and cost. Identifying patients who can achieve and sustain excellent glycemic control with optimized insulin regimens alone is crucial for personalized, cost-effective treatment. This addresses an inverse problem neglected in an era focused on additive therapy.
proposals.hypothesis: A distinct, identifiable phenotype exists among people with type 2 diabetes, characterized by specific clinical, metabolic, and glycemic variability profiles, that predicts sustained achievement of HbA1c <7.0% without hypoglycemia using modern basal insulin analogs (e.g., glargine 300 U/mL, degludec) and prandial insulin alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pooled, patient-level post-hoc analysis of Sanofi- and Lilly-sponsored cardiovascular outcomes trials (CVOTs) and CGM studies (e.g., InRange trial data) involving insulin-treated participants. Using pre-2026 machine learning methods, we will train a Random Forest classifier and a Cox proportional hazards model. The outcome is a composite of HbA1c <7.0% and no severe hypoglycemia over 1-2 years without adjunctive therapy. Predictors will include baseline demographics, diabetes duration, beta-cell function estimates (HOMA2), CGM-derived metrics (glucose variability, time-in-range), and concurrent CV/kidney risk factors.
