proposals.contribution: This work will provide a data-driven clinical profile to guide de-escalation or avoidance of unnecessary polypharmacy. It will establish evidence-based criteria for insulin monotherapy success, directly informing treatment guidelines and cost-benefit analyses. The findings will refocus precision medicine on identifying who needs less, not more, therapy.
proposals.novelty: The approach inverts the prevailing research question from 'who needs add-on therapy' to 'who does not,' leveraging existing CVOT and high-quality CGM data not originally mined for this purpose. It applies glycemic variability metrics from trials like InRange as predictive features for long-term monotherapy success.

proposals.title: A Multi-Objective Optimization Framework for Type 2 Diabetes Treatment: Prioritizing Hypoglycemia and Weight Safety over Glycemic Tightness in Clinical Decision Support
proposals.motivation: Recent cardiovascular outcomes trials (CVOTs) have shifted focus from pure glycemic control (HbA1c) to broader safety and cardiorenal outcomes. However, clinical algorithms and treatment intensification protocols remain largely efficacy-driven, often at the expense of hypoglycemia risk and weight gain, which impact quality of life and long-term adherence. There is a critical need for a formal, data-driven framework that explicitly treats safety (hypoglycemia, weight) as a primary objective, not just a constraint, potentially accepting modestly higher HbA1c for superior safety profiles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a treatment optimization algorithm explicitly designed to maximize a composite safety score (minimizing hypoglycemia events and weight gain) while maintaining glycemic control within a relaxed, personalized target range will yield protocols with superior patient-centered outcomes compared to standard efficacy-first protocols, without increasing long-term microvascular risk.
proposals.method: We will develop a reinforcement learning (RL) model using retrospective data from electronic health records (EHRs) and linked CGM data (e.g., from Abbott systems). The state space will include glucose variability metrics, weight trend, and treatment history. The action space will involve adjustments to second-generation basal insulins (e.g., Sanofi's Glargine 300 U/mL, Lilly's/Novo Nordisk's Degludec) and other agents. The reward function will be a weighted multi-objective function heavily penalizing hypoglycemic episodes (CGM-derived) and weight gain, while rewarding HbA1c maintenance within a personalized range (e.g., 7.0-8.0%). We will use an actor-critic algorithm (e.g., Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient) to learn optimal treatment policies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative framework for 'safety-first' treatment optimization in type 2 diabetes, directly addressing the dual problem. It will generate specific, evidence-based protocols that clinicians can use to personalize therapy goals. The methodology will also offer a blueprint for integrating CGM-derived safety metrics into automated decision support systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., InRange trial) compares drug safety profiles, this proposal innovates by algorithmically designing treatment protocols where safety is the primary optimization target, formally inverting the traditional efficacy-safety hierarchy. The use of RL with a multi-objective reward function tailored to this specific dual problem is novel in diabetes management.

proposals.title: Clinical Validation of an Electronic Health Record-Integrated Glycemic Risk Index (GRI) Dashboard and its Impact on Treatment Intensification and Glycemic Outcomes in Type 2 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Despite the availability of continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) metrics like Time in Range, their integration into routine clinical practice and electronic health records (EHRs) remains limited. The Glycemic Risk Index (GRI) is a composite CGM-derived metric that simplifies complex glucose variability data into a single actionable score, but its utility in real-world clinical decision support is untested. Implementing GRI within EHRs could bridge the gap between advanced CGM analytics and point-of-care treatment decisions, potentially improving outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implementing a GRI-based clinical decision support tool within the EHR will increase the rate of appropriate treatment intensification by clinicians and lead to improved glycemic control, as measured by a reduction in GRI and an increase in Time in Range, compared to standard care.
proposals.method: We will develop a SMART-on-FHIR application that integrates with the Epic EHR system. The application will pull CGM data (e.g., from Abbott LibreView or Dexcom Clarity) via standardized APIs, calculate the GRI using its published formula (incorporating hypoglycemia and hyperglycemia components), and display it on a dashboard within the patient chart alongside the GRI grid for risk stratification. In a 12-month, cluster-randomized controlled trial, primary care clinics will be assigned to either the intervention (GRI dashboard with alerts for high-risk patients) or control (standard EHR view). The primary outcome will be the proportion of patients with treatment intensification (e.g., dose adjustment, addition of agent like a GLP-1RA or SGLT2i from Lilly, Novo Nordisk, or Sanofi) within 90 days of a high-GRI alert.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospective evidence for the clinical utility of the GRI as an integrated decision support metric. It will demonstrate a practical model for embedding CGM analytics into EHR workflows, moving beyond retrospective review to proactive management. The findings could establish a new standard for leveraging digital health data to drive timely, data-driven treatment changes in diabetes care.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., the InRange trial) has compared GRI values between treatments, no study has yet operationalized the GRI grid within a live EHR environment to actively guide clinical decisions. This proposal moves from metric validation to implementation science, testing a closed-loop system from CGM data to therapeutic action.

proposals.title: Optimizing the Glycemic Risk-Return Portfolio: A Quantitative Framework for Evaluating Second-Generation Basal Insulin Analogs Using Continuous Glucose Monitoring Data
proposals.motivation: Modern diabetes management, particularly with second-generation basal insulins like glargine 300 U/mL and degludec 100 U/mL, aims to balance glycemic efficacy (return) against hypoglycemic risk. Current metrics like HbA1c and time-in-range are static and do not quantify the dynamic trade-off between these competing outcomes. A formal risk-return framework, analogous to financial portfolio optimization, is needed to provide a holistic, patient-centric evaluation of treatment strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a quantitative risk-return analysis, where glycemic exposure (e.g., mean glucose) is the 'return' and glycemic variability/hypoglycemia is the 'risk', will reveal distinct and clinically meaningful performance profiles for different insulin regimens, enabling a more nuanced optimization of diabetes management than standard metrics alone.
proposals.method: We will apply Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT) to retrospective CGM data from trials like InRange. For each patient/time-period, we will define daily 'return' as mean glucose and 'risk' as the standard deviation of glucose or a hypoglycemia-weighted metric (e.g., Low Blood Glucose Index). Using an efficient frontier algorithm, we will identify the theoretical optimal risk-return trade-off. We will then compare the actual performance of glargine 300 and degludec 100 against this frontier, assessing their relative efficiency and identifying patient factors (e.g., from DPPOS risk models) associated with suboptimal portfolio positioning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, unified quantitative framework for evaluating diabetes treatments, moving beyond isolated metrics. It will offer clinicians a visual and statistical tool to understand the inherent trade-offs in insulin therapy and could inform personalized treatment selection based on a patient's individual 'risk tolerance' for hypoglycemia.
proposals.novelty: While glycemic variability is recognized as a risk factor, this is the first application of formal financial portfolio optimization theory to model the explicit trade-off between glycemic control and hypoglycemia, creating a new paradigm for comparative effectiveness research in diabetes.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Short-Term, Rolling Glycemic Risk Index (GRI) for Real-Time Hypoglycemia and Hyperglycemia Alerts in Type 1 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: The Glycemic Risk Index (GRI) is a validated composite metric for assessing overall glycemic control, but it is calculated over weeks of CGM data, limiting its utility for immediate clinical intervention. Real-time risk prediction is crucial for preventing acute adverse events, especially as automated insulin delivery systems and connected insulin pens (e.g., from Sanofi, Lilly) become more prevalent. A short-term, dynamic risk metric could provide actionable warnings to patients and clinicians, potentially improving outcomes by enabling proactive management.
proposals.hypothesis: A rolling GRI, computed over a short-term window (e.g., 6-24 hours) of continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) data (e.g., from Abbott), will accurately predict impending hypoglycemic and hyperglycemic excursions and can be integrated into a real-time alert system to reduce time spent in extreme glycemic ranges.
proposals.method: We will adapt the standard GRI formula, which weights time below range (TBR) and time above range (TAR), to a rolling window (e.g., 6h) using high-frequency CGM data streams. The algorithm will compute the short-term GRI in near real-time, applying dynamic thresholds for risk severity derived from prior outcomes research (e.g., CVOT Summit themes). We will validate this approach using CGM data from existing RCTs (e.g., the InRange trial comparing glargine U300 and degludec) to correlate spikes in the rolling GRI with subsequent clinically significant events within the next 1-3 hours.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, validated metric for real-time glycemic risk assessment, moving beyond retrospective analysis. It will offer a framework for integrating immediate risk alerts into diabetes management software and connected devices, directly informing treatment decisions. This contributes to the field of personalized, data-driven diabetes care by translating a population-level outcomes metric into an individual patient tool.
proposals.novelty: While GRI is established for long-term evaluation, its adaptation to a real-time, rolling calculation for immediate risk prediction is novel. This approach differs from simple threshold-based CGM alarms by providing a composite, graded risk score that accounts for both hypoglycemia and hyperglycemia trends simultaneously, offering a more nuanced alert system.

proposals.title: A Reinforcement Learning Framework for Personalized Insulin Therapy Optimization to Minimize the Glycemic Risk Index (GRI)
proposals.motivation: The Glycemic Risk Index (GRI) is a composite CGM-derived metric that quantifies hyperglycemia, hypoglycemia, and glycemic variability into a single score, providing a holistic measure of glycemic quality. While GRI is a valuable outcome measure in trials like InRange, its clinical utility is limited without a clear pathway to translate a high GRI score into actionable therapy adjustments. Current clinical decision support systems for insulin dosing do not explicitly target GRI minimization, creating a gap between risk assessment and therapeutic optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model-based reinforcement learning (RL) algorithm, trained on retrospective continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) and insulin dosing data, can learn effective, personalized policies for adjusting basal and/or bolus insulin doses to predictively minimize a patient's future GRI.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage algorithm. First, a patient-specific physiological model (e.g., a neural network approximating a glucose-insulin model) will be trained on historical Abbott Freestyle Libre CGM and insulin pump/pen data to forecast glucose trajectories. Second, a reinforcement learning agent (using a policy gradient method like Proximal Policy Optimization) will interact with this model to learn insulin adjustment policies that optimize a reward function defined as the negative of forecasted GRI. The algorithm will be validated using a simulated patient cohort built on the FDA-accepted UVA/Padova T1D Simulator and then tested on a real-world dataset of individuals with T1D on second-generation basal insulins (e.g., glargine 300 U/mL, degludec).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first computational framework to directly translate the GRI metric into personalized insulin therapy recommendations, moving beyond descriptive risk scoring. It will contribute a novel decision-support tool that closes the loop from CGM-derived risk assessment to actionable treatment optimization, with potential applications in both automated insulin delivery systems and clinician-guided therapy adjustments.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has used RL for glucose control, targeting traditional metrics like Time-in-Range, this proposal uniquely formulates and solves the inverse problem of optimizing therapy explicitly for the composite GRI outcome. This integrates the multidimensional risk components of GRI into a single optimization objective, a non-obvious extension of existing glycemic control algorithms.

proposals.title: A Multi-Modal Data Fusion Approach to Enhance the Glycemic Risk Index (GRI) for Personalized Hypoglycemia and Hyperglycemia Risk Prediction in Type 1 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM)-derived metrics like the Glycemic Risk Index (GRI) provide a composite score of hypoglycemia and hyperglycemia risk but are inherently reactive. A holistic risk assessment requires integrating the exogenous factors that drive glucose variability. Pre-2026, the integration of insulin dose (from connected pens/pumps), meal logging (via apps), and physical activity (from wearables) with CGM data remains an underexplored opportunity for proactive risk prediction, which is critical for improving outcomes as highlighted in recent cardiovascular and renal outcomes trials (CVOTs).
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-modal data fusion model incorporating temporally aligned insulin, meal, and activity data with CGM will generate a significantly more accurate and anticipatory risk index than the standard GRI, enabling the prediction of impending glycemic excursions.
proposals.method: We will leverage data from a randomized controlled trial (e.g., InRange trial structure) where participants with T1D use CGM (e.g., Abbott FreeStyle Libre), connected insulin pens (e.g., Sanofi SoloSmart), and activity trackers. A time-series fusion architecture will be developed, using algorithms like Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks or attention-based transformers, to model the temporal relationships between insulin boluses, carbohydrate intake, metabolic equivalents (METs) from activity, and subsequent glucose values. The model will output a dynamic, multi-modal GRI (MM-GRI) score.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, actionable risk index that moves beyond description to prediction, directly informing personalized treatment adjustments. It will provide a novel methodology for integrating real-world data streams from devices by Sanofi, Lilly, and Abbott, contributing to the development of next-generation decision support systems aimed at reducing long-term complications.
proposals.novelty: While GRI is a static CGM summary metric, this proposal dynamically fuses it with causative multi-modal data streams to create a predictive tool. This approach is distinct from prior risk studies that often analyze modalities in isolation or correlate them with outcomes retrospectively.

proposals.title: Heterogeneity in Glycemic Target Efficacy: A Post-Hoc Analysis of Cardiovascular Outcome Trials to Stratify HbA1c Goal Applicability by Diabetes Subtype and Duration
proposals.motivation: The uniform HbA1c target of <7% is a cornerstone of diabetes management, derived from landmark trials like the UKPDS. However, subsequent cardiovascular outcome trials (CVOTs) for newer agents from Sanofi, Lilly, and others have enrolled heterogeneous populations, suggesting the benefit-risk profile of intensive control may vary. This one-size-fits-all target may not be meaningful for all patients, potentially obscuring optimal treatment strategies for specific subgroups.
proposals.hypothesis: The association between achieving an HbA1c <7% and improved composite cardiorenal outcomes differs significantly based on clinically defined diabetes subtypes (e.g., severe insulin-deficient, severe insulin-resistant) and disease duration, with the target showing greatest benefit in specific phenotypic clusters.
proposals.method: We will perform a pooled, patient-level, post-hoc analysis of major pre-2026 CVOTs (e.g., LEADER, REWIND, EMPA-REG OUTCOME) accessed through collaboration. Using established clustering variables (age at diagnosis, BMI, HOMA2 estimates, HbA1c), we will assign participants to diabetes subtypes. We will then use Cox proportional hazards models, stratified by trial, to test for interaction between achieved HbA1c (<7% vs. ≥7%), diabetes subtype/duration, and a composite cardiorenal outcome (CV death, non-fatal MI, stroke, hospitalization for heart failure, new macroalbuminuria). Machine learning techniques (random survival forests) will be used to validate interaction findings and explore non-linear relationships.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide evidence-based stratification to guide personalized glycemic goal setting, moving beyond a uniform target. It will inform clinical guidelines by identifying which patient phenotypes derive the strongest cardiorenal benefit from intensive glycemic control, and which may prioritize other risk factors.
proposals.novelty: While CVOTs have compared drug classes, no analysis has yet leveraged their pooled data to deconstruct the applicability of the common HbA1c target itself across modern diabetes subtypes. This directly relaxes the uniform target assumption using the most relevant contemporary outcome datasets.

proposals.title: A Retrospective Cohort Analysis of Factors Associated with Rising Prevalence of Poor Glycemic Control (HbA1c >9.0%) Among U.S. Adults with Diabetes, 2011-2014
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in diabetes pharmacotherapy, including second-generation basal insulin analogs and GLP-1 RAs, national data indicate an increase in the prevalence of very high HbA1c (>9.0%) from 2011-2014. This trend signals a critical public health gap and potential failure to translate clinical trial outcomes (CVOTs, DPPOS) and new technologies (CGM) into real-world glycemic management. Understanding the drivers of this increase is essential to inform targeted interventions and policy.
proposals.hypothesis: The observed increase in the prevalence of HbA1c >9.0% is primarily driven by a combination of socio-economic factors (e.g., insurance coverage changes, cost-related non-adherence), differential access to newer therapies and technologies (e.g., CGM, second-generation insulins), and shifts in clinical practice patterns following major cardiovascular outcome trial (CVOT) publications.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective longitudinal analysis using the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES) and linked pharmacy/insurance claims data (e.g., IBM MarketScan) from 2009-2016. The primary outcome is the probability of HbA1c >9.0%. We will employ a difference-in-differences framework to assess the impact of policy changes (e.g., ACA implementation) and a multi-variable, time-stratified logistic regression model with LASSO regularization for variable selection. Key predictors will include demographic/socioeconomic variables, diabetes type/duration, treatment regimen (using ATC codes for insulins like glargine 300 U/mL [Sanofi], degludec [Novo Nordisk], and GLP-1 RAs [Lilly, Novo Nordisk]), CGM use (Abbott FreeStyle Libre adoption), and comorbidity indices.
proposals.contribution: This study will identify modifiable risk factors and systemic barriers associated with worsening glycemic control in a real-world population, moving beyond efficacy demonstrated in trials like InRange. It will provide evidence to guide healthcare policy, resource allocation, and clinical strategies to reach high-risk patients, directly addressing the disconnect between therapeutic innovation and population-level outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., DPPOS) focuses on risk factors for diabetes onset, this analysis uniquely investigates determinants of extreme dysglycemia in the established diabetes population during a period of rapid therapeutic innovation, integrating clinical, pharmacologic, and socio-economic data streams to explain a counterintuitive negative trend.

proposals.title: Correlating Shifts in Antihyperglycemic Treatment Patterns with Population-Level HbA1c Trends: A Real-World Analysis from 2015-2024
proposals.motivation: Despite the introduction of advanced therapies with proven cardiovascular and renal benefits (e.g., SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 RAs), population-level glycemic control, as measured by HbA1c, has plateaued or worsened in some analyses pre-2026. It is critical to understand whether this trend is linked to specific patterns of medication adoption, persistence, and access, especially given the cost and formulary restrictions of newer agents. This analysis directly addresses the gap between clinical trial outcomes (CVOTs) and real-world effectiveness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the stagnation in population-level HbA1c target achievement is associated with identifiable lags and disparities in the real-world adoption of newer, more effective antihyperglycemic drug classes, and that these patterns vary significantly by insurance type and geographic care access.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective longitudinal cohort study using a large U.S. claims and linked laboratory database (e.g., Optum, IQVIA) from 2015-2024. Treatment patterns (initiation, switching, persistence) for drug classes (e.g., basal insulins like glargine 300 U/mL and degludec, GLP-1 RAs from Lilly and Novo Nordisk, SGLT2 inhibitors) will be quantified using established pharmacoepidemiologic measures (e.g., Proportion of Days Covered). These time-series patterns will be statistically correlated with concurrent, aggregated HbA1c trends using segmented regression and interrupted time-series analysis, with stratification by key covariates including payer (e.g., Medicare, commercial, Medicaid) and proxy measures for care access.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, quantitative link between real-world treatment adoption dynamics and macro-level glycemic outcomes. It will identify specific therapeutic classes and patient subgroups where treatment gaps most strongly correlate with suboptimal HbA1c, offering actionable insights for policymakers and health systems aiming to improve diabetes care quality and equity.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines either treatment patterns or HbA1c trends in isolation, this study innovatively correlates dynamic, class-level prescription data with population-level laboratory outcomes over a critical decade of therapeutic change, moving beyond simple descriptive statistics to test causal inference models of real-world effectiveness.

proposals.title: Geospatial Clustering of Glycemic Control in US Adults: A GIS Analysis of NHANES Data to Identify Hotspots of Elevated HbA1c
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in insulin analogs (e.g., glargine 300 U/mL, degludec) and continuous glucose monitoring, glycemic control remains suboptimal for many, contributing to long-term cardiovascular and kidney outcomes. Understanding the geographic distribution of poor control (HbA1c >7.0% or >8.0%) is critical for targeting public health interventions and resource allocation, yet prior analyses often lack spatial granularity. This directly addresses themes from the CVOT Summit 2022 on real-world glycemic outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that significant geographic clusters (hotspots) of elevated HbA1c exist within the United States, independent of individual-level demographic and clinical risk factors, and that these clusters are associated with area-level socioeconomic and healthcare access variables.
proposals.method: We will link publicly available National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES) data (cycles 2011-2020) with geographic identifiers (county/state) and Census tract-level socioeconomic data. Using Geographic Information Systems (GIS) software (ArcGIS Pro) and spatial statistics packages in R (spdep), we will perform spatial autocorrelation analysis (Global and Local Moran's I) to identify statistically significant clusters of high HbA1c. We will then use spatial regression models (e.g., geographically weighted regression) to examine associations with area-level covariates like poverty rate, food desert status, and primary care physician density.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the first national, spatially explicit map of glycemic control hotspots in the US adult population, identifying specific regions for targeted intervention. It will quantify the contribution of contextual, environmental factors to diabetes outcomes, complementing individual-level risk models. The findings will inform health policy and the deployment of resources by entities like Sanofi, Abbott, and Lilly, which focus on diabetes treatment and outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While NHANES is widely used for population health estimates, applying formal geospatial clustering techniques to its data to map HbA1c achievement is novel. This moves beyond reporting national or state averages to identify localized hotspots, integrating individual clinical data with community-level determinants in a spatially explicit framework.

proposals.title: Modeling the Diffusion of Second-Generation Insulin Analogs and GLP-1 RAs in the U.S. Population: A System Dynamics Approach to Explain Stagnant Glycemic and Cardiovascular Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Despite the introduction of superior second-generation basal insulins (e.g., glargine 300 U/mL, degludec) and GLP-1 receptor agonists with proven cardiovascular benefits, population-level diabetes outcomes remain stagnant. This disconnect suggests that the clinical trial efficacy demonstrated in CVOTs is not translating into real-world effectiveness at scale, likely due to slow and inequitable adoption of these innovations. Understanding the systemic barriers to treatment uptake is critical to improving public health impact.
proposals.hypothesis: The slow adoption of newer, more effective diabetes therapies, as modeled by diffusion of innovation theory, is a primary driver of stagnant population-level glycemic and cardiovascular outcomes, with adoption rates significantly influenced by payer coverage policies, prescriber inertia, and patient cost-sharing.
proposals.method: We will construct a system dynamics model integrating U.S. data sources (e.g., IQVIA prescription claims, Medicare/private insurer formularies, CDC NHANES). The model will parameterize a Bass Diffusion model for specific drug classes (e.g., GLP-1 RAs from Lilly/Novo Nordisk, basal insulin analogs from Sanofi/Novo Nordisk), where the coefficients of innovation and imitation are informed by real-world prescription data and formulary tier changes over time. This diffusion model will be linked to a simulated cohort model to project long-term glycemic (HbA1c) and cardiovascular outcomes, using risk equations calibrated to pre-2026 trial data (e.g., InRange, CVOT Summit reports).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework to explain the gap between clinical trial evidence and population health, identifying key leverage points (e.g., time to formulary inclusion, prescriber education) for accelerating beneficial diffusion. It will offer policymakers and health systems a simulation tool to forecast the health impact of coverage and reimbursement decisions for new diabetes therapies.
proposals.novelty: While diffusion models are common in marketing, their application to explain stagnant clinical outcomes in diabetes by linking prescription uptake dynamics to long-term health projections is novel. This approach moves beyond describing disparities to simulating the causal pathways of technological diffusion in a complex healthcare system.

proposals.title: Heterogeneity of Treatment Effects in Cardiovascular Outcome Trials: A Post-Hoc Analysis of High-Risk Subgroups with Uncontrolled Glycemia and Established Complications
proposals.motivation: While cardiovascular outcome trials (CVOTs) for diabetes therapies have established overall safety and benefit, their results represent population averages. Pre-2026 clinical practice recognizes that patients with very high baseline HbA1c or specific complications like chronic kidney disease constitute a high-risk, high-burden subgroup. The treatment effect trends and safety profiles of newer agents (e.g., SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 RAs) may differ meaningfully in these subgroups, but granular, dedicated analyses are often lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that within major diabetes CVOTs, the relative and absolute cardiovascular and renal benefits of newer glucose-lowering agents are significantly amplified in high-risk subgroups defined by very high baseline HbA1c (≥9.0%) or the presence of multiple microvascular complications, compared to the overall trial population.
proposals.method: We will conduct a focused, post-hoc analysis of patient-level data from major, pre-2026 CVOTs (e.g., DECLARE-TIMI 58, REWIND, EMPA-REG OUTCOME). High-risk subgroups will be defined a priori: 1) Baseline HbA1c ≥9.0%, and 2) Presence of ≥2 microvascular complications (retinopathy, neuropathy, nephropathy). Primary analysis will use Cox proportional hazards models with treatment-by-subgroup interaction terms to test for heterogeneity of treatment effect on 3-point MACE and hospitalization for heart failure. Secondary analyses will evaluate trends in glycemic efficacy (HbA1c change) and hypoglycemia risk within these subgroups.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide actionable evidence for personalizing therapy in the most challenging-to-treat patients, quantifying the potential for greater absolute risk reduction. It will inform guideline updates by clarifying whether high baseline risk should influence the strength of recommendation for specific drug classes. The analysis will also highlight if safety signals, like diabetic ketoacidosis with SGLT2 inhibitors, are concentrated in these subgroups.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond the published primary and subgroup analyses of individual trials, this proposal synthesizes data across multiple CVOTs to perform a targeted, hypothesis-driven investigation of the highest-risk phenotypes, an approach not systematically undertaken in the pre-2026 literature. It explicitly links extreme dysglycemia with complication burden to test for a gradient of treatment benefit.

proposals.title: Immunogenicity of Second-Generation Basal Insulin Analogs: A Post-Hoc Analysis of the InRange Trial Stratified by Baseline Anti-Insulin Antibody Status and High-Risk HLA Haplotypes
proposals.motivation: Immunogenicity remains a clinically relevant concern in insulin therapy, potentially impacting pharmacokinetics, glycemic variability, and treatment outcomes. While second-generation analogs like glargine 300 U/mL and degludec 100 U/mL show low immunogenicity overall, pre-2026 evidence suggests patient-specific factors like pre-existing antibodies or specific HLA haplotypes may influence antibody development. Understanding this heterogeneity is crucial for personalized treatment, especially as highlighted by recent CVOTs emphasizing comprehensive outcome assessments beyond HbA1c.
proposals.hypothesis: In patients with type 1 diabetes, high baseline levels of anti-insulin antibodies or the presence of specific high-risk HLA class II haplotypes (e.g., DRB1*04:01, DQB1*03:02) are associated with a greater magnitude of antibody response and increased glucose variability during treatment with second-generation basal insulin analogs.
proposals.method: We will conduct a post-hoc analysis of the InRange randomized controlled trial (CGM data for glargine 300 vs. degludec 100 in T1D). Baseline serum samples will be analyzed for anti-insulin antibody titers using validated radioimmunoassays. High-resolution HLA typing for DRB1 and DQB1 loci will be performed via PCR-based sequencing. The primary outcome is the change in antibody titer from baseline to 6 months. Secondary outcomes include CGM-derived metrics of glucose variability (coefficient of variation, standard deviation). Statistical analysis will use multivariable linear regression, adjusting for treatment arm, age, and diabetes duration, with interaction terms to test for effect modification by HLA haplotype.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed immunogenicity profile of modern basal insulins in genetically and immunologically defined subpopulations, moving beyond average treatment effects. It will inform clinical risk stratification by identifying patients who may require closer monitoring for antibody-mediated effects on glycemic control, contributing to more precise diabetes management.
proposals.novelty: While InRange compared glucose variability between analogs, it did not stratify by immunogenetic risk factors. This proposal integrates detailed immunophenotyping and HLA genotyping with high-resolution CGM outcomes, a novel approach to explain inter-individual variability in response to these specific therapies.

proposals.title: Long-term Immunogenicity, Glycemic Outcomes, and Cardiovascular Risk Following Treatment with Second-Generation Basal Insulin Analogs: A 5-Year Follow-up of the InRange Cohort
proposals.motivation: While second-generation basal insulin analogs (glargine 300 U/mL, degludec 100 U/mL) offer improved pharmacokinetics and reduced hypoglycemia risk, their long-term immunogenic potential and clinical impact remain underexplored. Pre-2026 evidence from CVOTs highlights the critical link between diabetes treatment and cardiovascular/renal outcomes, yet the role of anti-insulin antibodies (AIAs) in modulating long-term glycemic control and cardiometabolic risk is unclear, particularly in real-world settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sustained elevation of anti-insulin antibody (AIA) titers over 5 years of treatment with second-generation basal insulins is associated with increased glucose variability, attenuated HbA1c reduction, and a higher composite risk of cardiovascular and renal events, independent of traditional risk factors.
proposals.method: This is a prospective, observational 5-year follow-up of participants from the InRange RCT, leveraging linked data from continuous glucose monitoring (CGM, Abbott Freestyle Libre), electronic health records, and national registries. Serum AIA levels will be quantified annually using validated immunoassays. The primary analysis will employ time-varying Cox proportional hazards models to assess the association between AIA trajectory (modeled via latent class growth analysis) and a composite cardiorenal outcome (hospitalization for heart failure, myocardial infarction, stroke, or new macroalbuminuria). Secondary analyses will use linear mixed models to relate AIA levels to CGM-derived metrics (e.g., coefficient of variation, time-in-range) and HbA1c.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal data on the immunogenicity profile of modern basal insulins over a clinically relevant timeframe and establish whether AIAs are a novel, modifiable risk factor for adverse long-term outcomes. It will inform personalized treatment strategies by identifying patient subgroups who may benefit from immunogenicity monitoring or alternative therapies.
proposals.novelty: Existing immunogenicity studies are short-term (<2 years) and lack linkage to hard clinical endpoints. This proposal uniquely integrates long-term immunogenicity data with robust, device-measured glycemic outcomes (CGM) and adjudicated cardiorenal events, a synthesis not yet performed in pre-2026 research.

proposals.title: Clinical and Immunologic Outcomes Following a Non-Medical Switch from Insulin Glargine U-100 (Lantus) to its Biosimilar (Semglee) in a High-Risk Type 2 Diabetes Population: A Real-World Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: The increasing adoption of biosimilar insulins, such as insulin glargine-yfgn (Semglee), for cost-saving presents a critical evidence gap regarding outcomes after a non-medical switch from the originator (Lantus). Pre-2026, while regulatory approval confirms similarity, real-world data on glycemic control, hypoglycemia risk, and immunogenicity in high-risk patients with established cardiovascular or kidney disease are sparse. This is crucial as this population was a focus of recent CVOTs and may be more vulnerable to clinical instability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a non-medical switch from originator to biosimilar insulin glargine will be non-inferior in terms of glycemic control (HbA1c) but may be associated with a measurable increase in anti-insulin antibodies and a higher rate of severe hypoglycemia events in the first 6 months post-switch.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, propensity-score matched cohort study using linked electronic health records and pharmacy claims data from a large U.S. health system (2019-2024). The cohort will include adults with T2D and established CVD/CKD who switched to biosimilar glargine (exposed) versus those who remained on originator glargine (control). Primary outcomes are change in HbA1c and time to severe hypoglycemia (ICD-10 codes). A nested immunologic sub-study will analyze stored serum samples for anti-insulin antibody titers (using validated ELISA) in a matched subset pre- and 6-months post-switch. Statistical analysis will employ mixed-effects models and Cox proportional hazards, with non-inferiority margins defined a priori.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first real-world evidence pairing clinical outcomes with immunologic data for a biosimilar insulin switch in a high-risk population. It will inform payers, clinicians, and regulatory bodies on the safety profile of non-medical switching, potentially identifying a subpopulation requiring closer monitoring. The findings will directly address concerns raised in forums like the CVOT Summit regarding translating trial efficacy into real-world effectiveness for novel treatment strategies.
proposals.novelty: While InRange and others compared next-generation analogs, this focuses on the first-generation originator-to-biosimilar switch. The novelty lies in integrating large-scale clinical outcomes data with direct measurement of immunogenicity, a combination not yet explored in real-world biosimilar insulin studies pre-2026.

proposals.title: Long-Term Glycemic Trajectory Analysis of Second-Generation Basal Insulins: A Post-Hoc CGM Study of the InRange Trial Beyond 16 Weeks
proposals.motivation: Randomized controlled trials (RCTs) comparing second-generation basal insulin analogs, such as Sanofi's Glargine 300 U/mL and Novo Nordisk's Degludec 100 U/mL, often use a 16-week period to assess glycemic stabilization and primary endpoints. This timeframe, established in trials like InRange, is pragmatic but may not capture long-term, clinically relevant divergence in glycemic patterns, particularly in metrics like glucose variability (GV) and time-in-range (TIR). Given the established link between GV and microvascular complications, and the focus on long-term outcomes in CVOTs, understanding the durability of early differences is critical for personalized treatment decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that glycemic control metrics, specifically glucose variability and time-in-range, continue to diverge between treatment arms beyond the standard 16-week RCT period, with one analog demonstrating superior long-term stability.
proposals.method: We propose a post-hoc, longitudinal analysis of continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) data from the InRange RCT (NCT03514543) for participants who continued in open-label extension phases. Using mixed-effects models for repeated measures, we will analyze daily and weekly CGM-derived metrics (e.g., coefficient of variation, TIR, time below range) over a 52-week period. We will employ segmented regression or functional data analysis techniques to identify inflection points where glycemic trajectories stabilize or diverge, adjusting for baseline covariates (e.g., prior insulin regimen, HbA1c) and treatment adherence data.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide evidence-based guidance on the optimal duration for assessing comparative effectiveness of basal insulins in RCTs. It will inform clinical practice by indicating whether early (16-week) glycemic patterns are predictive of long-term control, thereby impacting treatment escalation decisions and personalized diabetes management strategies.
proposals.novelty: While InRange and similar trials reported 16-week outcomes, no pre-2026 analysis has systematically examined the long-term CGM trajectory of these specific analogs head-to-head to challenge the sufficiency of this standard stabilization period for capturing meaningful clinical differences.

proposals.title: Comparative Real-World Effectiveness and Safety of Second-Generation Basal Insulin Analogs in High-Risk Subpopulations: A Focus on the Elderly and Those with Prior Severe Hypoglycemia
proposals.motivation: While second-generation basal insulin analogs (glargine 300 U/mL, degludec 100 U/mL) offer improved pharmacokinetic profiles, their comparative efficacy and safety in clinically important, high-risk subgroups remain inadequately characterized in real-world practice. Elderly patients and those with a history of severe hypoglycemia represent vulnerable populations where treatment decisions must balance glycemic control with profound safety risks, yet evidence from randomized controlled trials (RCTs) like InRange may not fully generalize to these heterogeneous real-world cohorts.
proposals.hypothesis: In high-risk subpopulations (age ≥75 years and those with prior severe hypoglycemia), treatment with second-generation basal insulin analogs will demonstrate differential real-world outcomes, with one analog showing a superior safety profile (reduced hypoglycemia-related hospitalizations) while maintaining comparable glycemic control as measured by time-in-range.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, propensity-score-matched cohort study using a large U.S. claims database (e.g., Optum Clinformatics) linked to laboratory results and Abbott Freestyle Libre CGM data (where available). High-risk cohorts will be defined as: 1) age ≥75, 2) prior severe hypoglycemia event (ED visit/hospitalization). Patients initiating glargine 300 U/mL or degludec 100 U/mL will be matched 1:1 within each subgroup. Primary outcomes will be severe hypoglycemia events (claims-based) and glycemic control (change in HbA1c, CGM-derived time-in-range). We will use Cox proportional hazards and generalized linear mixed models, with pre-specified interaction tests to formally assess treatment effect heterogeneity by subgroup.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide crucial, actionable evidence for personalized insulin selection in high-risk populations, directly informing clinical guidelines and payer formularies. It will extend findings from RCTs like InRange into real-world, high-complexity patients and establish a framework for evaluating treatment effect heterogeneity using real-world data, a key focus of contemporary outcomes research.
proposals.novelty: This proposal moves beyond average treatment effects from RCTs to explicitly test for heterogeneous treatment effects in pre-specified, clinically critical subgroups using a linked claims-CGM data asset, a methodologically rigorous approach not commonly applied to comparative insulin studies pre-2026.

proposals.title: Inferring Basal Insulin Type from Continuous Glucose Monitoring Patterns: A Machine Learning Approach
proposals.motivation: Second-generation basal insulin analogs (e.g., Sanofi's Glargine U300, Lilly's Basaglar, Novo Nordisk's Degludec) exhibit distinct pharmacodynamic profiles, influencing glucose variability and hypoglycemia risk. Current clinical practice relies on self-report or pharmacy records to ascertain insulin type, which can be inaccurate. A method to objectively infer the insulin type from CGM data would enable verification of real-world treatment adherence and facilitate more precise retrospective analysis of outcomes in large observational datasets.
proposals.hypothesis: The pharmacodynamic signatures of different basal insulin analogs generate unique, quantifiable patterns in CGM-derived metrics, enabling machine learning models to classify the specific insulin type with accuracy exceeding chance.
proposals.method: We will train supervised learning models on CGM traces from randomized controlled trials like InRange, where insulin type is known. Feature engineering will extract metrics on central tendency, variability (e.g., CV, LBGI), time-in-ranges, and patterns of hypoglycemia. We will compare the performance of traditional classifiers (e.g., Random Forest, XGBoost) against a recurrent neural network (RNN) architecture, specifically Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, to capture temporal dependencies in the raw glucose time series. Models will be validated on held-out trial data and tested for generalizability using real-world CGM datasets from sources like Abbott's LibreView platform.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first computational tool for non-invasive insulin type identification, creating a new method for treatment verification in real-world evidence studies. It will also generate novel insights into the distinct glycemic 'fingerprints' of modern basal insulins, linking specific CGM patterns to pharmacological properties.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., InRange trial) compares insulin effects on CGM outcomes, the inverse problem—deducing the treatment from the outcome signal—is unexplored. Applying sequence models like LSTMs to this classification task represents a novel application of machine learning in diabetes therapeutics.

proposals.title: Quantifying Individual Glycemic Risk: A Value-at-Risk (VaR) Framework for Continuous Glucose Monitoring Data in Type 1 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Glycemic variability, particularly hypoglycemia, poses significant clinical and quality-of-life risks for people with diabetes. Current metrics like standard deviation or time-in-range are descriptive but do not quantify the personal probability of future adverse glycemic events. This limits personalized risk communication and treatment optimization, especially as new insulin analogs (e.g., Sanofi's Glargine 300 U/mL, Lilly/Novo Nordisk's Degludec) and CGM systems (e.g., Abbott) generate high-frequency data.
proposals.hypothesis: Financial Value-at-Risk (VaR) models, adapted to CGM data, can robustly estimate an individual's personalized probability of exceeding critical glycemic thresholds (e.g., <54 mg/dL or >250 mg/dL) over a defined future period, and this risk metric will correlate with established clinical outcomes and patient-reported fear of hypoglycemia.
proposals.method: We will analyze CGM data from trials like InRange, applying historical simulation and parametric VaR methods. The core algorithm will treat glucose time series as a 'risk asset,' calculating the glucose level (VaR) that will not be exceeded with a specified confidence level (e.g., 95%) over a 24-hour 'holding period.' We will model tails of the glucose distribution separately for hypo- and hyperglycemia. The framework will be validated against real-world outcomes data (e.g., from CVOT studies) and compared to traditional variability metrics using regression models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, intuitive risk metric—'Your daily 5% risk glucose level is 58 mg/dL'—that translates complex CGM data into actionable information for clinicians and patients. It will establish a formal link between quantitative finance and diabetes informatics, creating a tool for evaluating the 'risk reduction' impact of new treatments (e.g., from Lilly, Sanofi) in a patient-centric manner.
proposals.novelty: While financial risk models are standard in economics, their application to physiological time-series data in diabetes is novel. This moves beyond describing past variability to forecasting personalized probabilistic risk, a conceptual shift from descriptive statistics to predictive risk modeling for daily diabetes management.

ideas.title: Longitudinal Investigation of Dynamic Barriers and Glycemic Trajectories in Emerging Adults with Type 1 Diabetes
ideas.description: This study will recruit a cohort of young adults (18-25) with Type 1 diabetes and the highest baseline HbA1c, then follow them intensively over 5+ years. It employs mixed methods, combining frequent ecological momentary assessments (EMAs) and quarterly in-depth interviews to capture real-time, evolving barriers to self-care (psychosocial, systemic, behavioral). These longitudinal barrier profiles are then quantitatively linked to frequently measured glycemic control (CGM/HbA1c) and early biomarkers of complications, allowing us to model how specific barrier patterns (e.g., instability during life transitions) predict both glycemic volatility and the accelerated onset of complication pathways.

ideas.title: Evaluating Diabetes Technology as an Effect Modifier in the HbA1c-Complication Relationship
ideas.description: This research adapts the methodological approach from vaccine efficacy studies, where vaccination status is analyzed as an effect modifier of the pathogen-disease relationship. The analogous solution involves using interaction terms or stratified models to quantify how an intervention changes a biological risk relationship. Adapted to diabetes, the idea is to analyze how Continuous Glucose Monitor (CGM) or insulin pump use modifies the relationship between HbA1c and complication rates (e.g., retinopathy, nephropathy). Instead of just comparing mean HbA1c between user groups, the study would formally test for interaction, determining if the technology attenuates the hazard ratio or odds ratio linking a given HbA1c level to complication risk, thereby quantifying its role as a protective effect modifier.

ideas.title: Disentangling the Bidirectional Causal Loop Between Glycemic Control and Early Complications
ideas.description: This research employs a causal inference framework to rigorously model the bidirectional relationship between glycemic control and diabetes complications, moving beyond simple prediction. Using longitudinal patient data and time-series models, it quantifies the two primary pathways: the established effect of hyperglycemia on complication risk, and the hypothesized feedback effect where early signs of complications may degrade subsequent glycemic control through mechanisms like changed self-care or altered physiology. The outcome is a validated structural model that identifies if, when, and for whom this feedback loop is operational, providing a robust basis for targeted interventions to break the cycle.

ideas.title: Leveraging Enhanced Complication Detection to Drive and Personalize Interventions for Clinical Inertia in Diabetes Management
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the investigation of EHR data completeness for diabetes complications with the direct testing of interventions for clinical inertia. The core idea is to first develop and validate a method (e.g., using natural language processing on clinical notes) to more accurately identify early signs of complications like neuropathy or retinopathy that are missed by structured diagnosis codes. This enhanced, real-time complication detection data is then integrated into a clinical decision support tool specifically designed to overcome inertia in insulin therapy. The tool would not just remind clinicians to consider insulin, but would present patient-specific, complication-derived evidence of disease progression, creating a powerful, personalized impetus for timely treatment intensification. The study would evaluate whether this data-driven, complication-aware intervention is more effective at reducing inertia and improving glycemic outcomes than standard reminder systems.

ideas.title: Estimate Heterogeneous Causal Effects of Glycemic Control Using Causal Machine Learning and Multiple Quasi-Experimental Designs
ideas.description: This research moves beyond estimating an average causal effect by using causal machine learning methods to discover and estimate how the effect of HbA1c on diabetes complications varies across different patient profiles. The system integrates instrumental variables, difference-in-differences, and other quasi-experimental designs as inputs to a flexible HTE estimation model, allowing for robustness checks across identification strategies. It explicitly tests for modification of the HbA1c effect by factors like diabetes duration, age, comorbidities, and medication use. The output is a set of personalized causal effect estimates and validated subgroups for whom intensive glycemic control is most or least beneficial, providing a more nuanced and clinically actionable evidence base.

ideas.title: Complication-Informed Glycemic Phenotyping: Using Early Tissue Damage to Deconstruct HbA1c and Predict Trajectories
ideas.description: This research challenges the sufficiency of HbA1c by proposing that early signs of tissue complications (e.g., retinal changes, autonomic dysfunction, or albuminuria) contain latent information about the specific glycemic patterns that produced them. By modeling these early complications as an 'inverse problem,' we can infer the historical glycemic exposures—such as glycemic variability or nocturnal hypoglycemia—that are masked by a single HbA1c average. This complication-informed phenotyping allows for the stratification of patients with similar HbA1c into distinct risk groups and creates predictive models where early complication signatures forecast imminent loss of glycemic stability, enabling pre-emptive intervention.

ideas.title: Developmental Trajectory Modeling: Predicting Glycemic Control Deterioration from Early Complications in Pediatric T1D
ideas.description: This study investigates the inverse problem within the pediatric and adolescent type 1 diabetes population, specifically examining whether early signs of diabetes-related complications (e.g., mild hypoglycemia unawareness, early microalbuminuria, or specific psychosocial stressors) can predict subsequent loss of glycemic control. By focusing on this developmental period, the research will analyze how these predictive signals are modulated by key maturational variables such as puberty, growth velocity, and cognitive development. The goal is to create dynamic, age-specific risk models that identify children at highest risk for control deterioration before it becomes clinically evident. This enables targeted, developmentally appropriate interventions aimed at preserving long-term glycemic health during critical windows of growth.

ideas.title: The Pediatric T1D Ecosystem Study: A Developmentally-Embedded Causal Inference Platform
ideas.description: This study establishes a longitudinal, hybrid registry-intervention platform specifically for children and adolescents with type 1 diabetes, co-designed with patients, families, and care teams to ensure ethical and practical feasibility. It integrates continuous passive data from devices (CGMs, insulin pens/pumps) with periodic, developmentally-tailored active assessments from both the youth and their caregivers to capture contextual and psychosocial factors. Using advanced causal inference methodologies on this rich, real-world dataset, the research identifies stage-specific, modifiable drivers of glycemic outcomes and acute complications. The platform is designed to generate immediately actionable insights for personalized care strategies while maintaining a sustainable framework for long-term follow-up through key developmental transitions.

ideas.title: Disentangling Diagnostic Label from Disease Biology: A Target Trial for Algorithm Misclassification in Diabetes
ideas.description: This study employs a target trial emulation to isolate the effect of an EHR algorithm's diagnostic misclassification from the inherent complication risks of the underlying diabetes type. We construct three matched cohorts: algorithm-T1D, gold-standard confirmed T1D, and a phenotypically similar algorithm-T2D group that mirrors likely misclassified cases. By comparing complication rates across these groups, we can separately estimate the 'label effect' of incorrect classification (e.g., care pathway deviations) and the 'biological effect' of the actual disease pathophysiology, providing a causally rigorous assessment of the algorithm's clinical impact.

ideas.title: Mapping the Real-Time Dynamics and Contexts of Glycemic Control Failure in Emerging Adults with Diabetes
ideas.description: This study investigates the causes of poor glycemic control in 18-25 year-olds by capturing the dynamic, in-the-moment processes that lead to control failures. Using smartphone-based ecological momentary assessment (EMA), continuous glucose monitor (CGM) data, and passive sensing, it collects real-time data on behavior, context, stress, and decision-making alongside glucose values. These quantitative temporal patterns are then explored and explained through subsequent in-depth phenomenological interviews, creating a rich, actionable model of the interacting individual and environmental factors that undermine control.

ideas.title: Apply Mendelian Randomization from Genetic Epidemiology to Estimate Causal Effect of HbA1c
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core solution from genetic epidemiology—Mendelian Randomization—to the problem of estimating the causal effect of HbA1c on diabetes complications. In genetic epidemiology, Mendelian Randomization uses genetic variants as instrumental variables to infer causality between modifiable exposures and health outcomes, capitalizing on the random allocation of alleles to minimize confounding. The adapted research idea applies this framework by identifying specific genetic variants (e.g., in the GCK, G6PC2, or HK1 genes) known to be robustly associated with HbA1c levels from genome-wide association studies. These variants serve as natural, randomly assigned instruments. By analyzing their association with complication rates in large biobanks or cohort studies (like UK Biobank), one can estimate the causal effect of glycemic exposure, providing stronger evidence than standard observational associations and circumventing issues of reverse causality and unmeasured confounding like lifestyle factors.

ideas.title: Create the inverse problem: optimize therapy from GRI

ideas.title: Applying Behavioral Nudge Theory from Public Health to Identify and Overcome Glycemic Control Barriers
ideas.description: This research seed idea is expanded via analogy to public health and behavioral economics. The analogous problem is poor adherence to health protocols in general populations, which is often addressed using nudge theory to identify behavioral bottlenecks and design subtle choice architecture interventions. Adapted to diabetes research, the project will first conduct a behavioral audit to map the specific decision points and environmental/psychological barriers (e.g., transition stress, complexity of management, social stigma) that degrade glycemic control in the 18-25 age group, who exhibit the highest HbA1c. Subsequently, it will develop and pilot-test a suite of digitally-delivered, age-appropriate behavioral nudges—informed by cognitive science and tailored to their daily routines and technology use—aimed at reducing friction in self-care and improving long-term glycemic outcomes.

ideas.title: Causal Estimation of the Net Benefit of Personalized Glycemic Titration Delays
ideas.description: This research applies causal inference methods from econometrics, such as instrumental variables or regression discontinuity, to estimate the effect of a 'delay in HbA1c reduction' on a personalized composite endpoint. The endpoint integrates traditional complication risks with patient-centered outcomes like treatment burden, hypoglycemia, and quality of life. This allows for a formal, data-driven test of the hypothesis that for some patient phenotypes, a deliberate titration delay is causally associated with superior overall well-being compared to standard timely intensification, thereby reframing delay from 'suboptimal care' to a potentially optimal personalized strategy.

ideas.title: A Cross-System, Cross-Cultural Analysis of Insulin Titration Attitudes Using Ecological Framework Adaptation
ideas.description: This research adapts a methodological solution from cultural ecology to the original idea of comparing insulin titration attitudes across healthcare systems. In cultural ecology, comparative studies analyze how different environmental systems shape local practices and beliefs. The analogous solution involves creating a framework that quantifies key environmental variables and tests their relationship to cultural outcomes. Adapted to this context, the research would first define and operationalize key variables of the healthcare 'ecosystem' (e.g., payment structure, guideline enforcement, digital health infrastructure, specialist-to-patient ratios) in a diverse set of countries. A replicated survey would capture perceptions of insulin titration among patients and providers. The core analysis would then use multivariate modeling to determine which specific systemic factors most strongly predict attitudes like self-efficacy, fear of hypoglycemia, or perceived complexity, thereby revealing the mechanistic influence of system design on clinical behavior and patient experience.

ideas.title: Longitudinal Causal Pathway Analysis: Linking Titration Adherence Dynamics to Long-Term Macrovascular Risk
ideas.description: This study prospectively tracks a cohort of patients, repeatedly measuring HCP and patient attitudes, actual titration behavior, and glycemic outcomes over time. It extends follow-up to capture incident macrovascular complications and mortality, stratified by age and HbA1c. The core synergy is using longitudinal structural equation modeling to test how dynamic patterns of adherence mediate the relationship between baseline attitudes and long-term cardiovascular risk, moving beyond correlation to identify high-leverage behavioral intervention points for preventing complications.

ideas.title: Applying Reliability Engineering's Failure Time Analysis to EHR for Complication Onset
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core methodology from reliability engineering, where survival analysis models the time-to-failure of components under stress, to the clinical prediction of diabetic complications. The analogous solution uses parametric survival models with censored data to identify accelerating factors. Adapted to EHR data, the research would model the 'time-to-complication' from a high HbA1c measurement, employing Accelerated Failure Time models that can incorporate longitudinal, time-varying patient data (e.g., lab results, prescriptions) as covariates. This provides a dynamic, individualized prediction of complication risk over time, moving beyond a simple binary outcome to estimate the expected timing of the event.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismological Hazard Models to Map Glycemic Instability and Complication Risk
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from seismological hazard forecasting, this study proposes to track a diabetes cohort longitudinally, not just for mean glycemic levels (HbA1c), but for high-frequency, time-series data on glycemic excursions and variability via continuous glucose monitoring (CGM). In seismology, patterns of small precursor events predict major earthquakes. Here, patterns of glycemic 'foreshocks' (e.g., frequency of hyperglycemic spikes, duration of swings, hypoglycemic episodes) will be analyzed as predictors. The adapted solution is to use statistical survival and hazard modeling techniques, similar to seismic hazard maps, to generate personalized risk trajectories. The outcome is a dynamic model that forecasts the timing and type of complication onset (retinopathy, nephropathy) based on the evolving 'seismic activity' of an individual's glucose control, moving beyond static, snapshot risk assessments.

ideas.title: Goal-Conflict-Aware Predictive Modeling of Glycemic Control
ideas.description: This research investigates whether early signs of complications can predict subsequent loss of glycemic control, but crucially, it models this relationship within the context of potential goal conflicts between healthcare providers (HCPs) and patients. The model will identify predictive biomarkers for control deterioration and simultaneously estimate the associated risks to patient-prioritized outcomes, such as quality of life or treatment burden. This allows the generation of personalized forecasts that illustrate the trade-off between aggressive control (an HCP priority) and avoidance of negative impacts on patient-defined goals. The result is a clinical decision-support tool that makes latent goal conflicts explicit, facilitating communication and shared decision-making based on a transparent, evidence-based projection of outcomes.

ideas.title: Pragmatic Digital Phenotyping and Real-Time Context Capture of Titration Behavior
ideas.description: This study embeds longitudinal observation within routine diabetes care to objectively link real-world titration actions with their immediate contextual drivers. It uses passive digital phenotyping—integrating data from smart insulin pens, continuous glucose monitors, and pharmacy records—to establish a precise, objective timeline of medication use and glycemic outcomes. This behavioral timeline is enriched with ecological momentary assessments triggered by specific events (e.g., a missed dose or a persistent hyperglycemic pattern) to capture cognitive, emotional, and environmental factors in real time. The resulting system moves beyond correlating static attitudes with outcomes, instead building a causal model of the decision architecture underlying titration adherence by directly observing the sequence of events, contexts, and thoughts that lead to clinical actions.

ideas.title: Adapting Pediatric Drug Dosing Models to Real-World Pediatric T1D Studies
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from pediatric clinical pharmacology to the challenge of scaling a real-world evidence study down to a pediatric T1D population. In drug development, physiologically-based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) modeling is used to create age-stratified pediatric dosing models by integrating known developmental physiology, rather than simply scaling adult doses. Adapted to this context, the research would develop a pediatric-specific real-world study framework for T1D. This framework would systematically integrate developmental stage parameters (e.g., puberty-related insulin resistance, cognitive capacity for self-management) and pediatric-specific environmental factors (school routines, parental supervision levels) into the study's design, data collection tools, and analytical models. The result is a tailored observational study that accurately reflects the distinct physiology, behavior, and outcomes of children and adolescents with T1D, moving beyond a mere size reduction of an adult study.

ideas.description: This study investigates how misclassification of diabetes type by EHR algorithms affects both clinical outcomes (e.g., complication rates) and clinician attitudes (e.g., trust in algorithms, insulin titration practices) across diverse healthcare systems. By comparing data from countries with varying levels of digital integration, resource availability, and clinical guidelines, the research will determine if the impact of algorithmic error is universal or system-dependent. This synergy reveals how technological imperfections interact with systemic human factors to shape overall care quality and technology adoption.

ideas.title: Assess macrovascular complications and mortality using a structural reliability engineering framework
ideas.description: This research adapts the problem-solving framework from structural engineering reliability analysis to assess macrovascular complications and mortality in a diabetic cohort. The analogous solution involves modeling system failure under continuous stress, which is adapted by treating the cardiovascular system as a structure, chronic hyperglycemia (HbA1c) as the primary stressor, and age as a proxy for cumulative exposure time. The adapted idea is to employ engineering reliability methods—specifically, survival analysis and competing risks models—to estimate the probability and timing of specific cardiovascular 'component failures' (e.g., myocardial infarction, stroke) and ultimate 'system collapse' (death). The analysis will be stratified by the same age and HbA1c groups to identify critical risk thresholds, mirroring how engineers stratify risk by material property and environmental exposure to predict structural failure.

ideas.title: Incidence and EHR Capture of Early Complications in Newly Diagnosed Adults
ideas.description: This fused research direction investigates the incidence and electronic health record (EHR) data capture of complications like neuropathy and retinopathy specifically in adults newly diagnosed with a chronic disease. By relaxing the traditional ≥24-month disease duration requirement, the study captures the early disease phase, while concurrently validating and quantifying the undercounting of complications in EHR codes through chart review or natural language processing. This combined approach uniquely determines whether low reported complication rates in early disease are due to genuine low incidence or systematic data capture failure, providing critical insights for early intervention strategies and real-world evidence study design.

ideas.title: Bias-Aware, Fairness-Constrained Ensemble Learning for Equitable Diabetic Crisis Risk Stratification
ideas.description: This research develops an ensemble machine learning system for predicting severe hypoglycemia (SH) and diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) that explicitly prioritizes algorithmic fairness and equity. The core innovation is a multi-stage modeling pipeline where predictive performance is balanced with bias mitigation through adversarial debiasing and fairness constraints during training. The system continuously audits and reports model performance disparities across racial, ethnic, socioeconomic, and gender subgroups. Furthermore, it incorporates a causal analysis step to identify and adjust for non-clinical confounders in EHR data, ensuring the final risk scores are clinically actionable and equitable across diverse patient populations.

ideas.title: Multi-State Cohort Study with Registry-Linked Endpoint Ascertainment to Correct Attrition Bias in Complication Trajectories
ideas.description: This study establishes a prospective cohort with initial deep phenotyping but moves beyond standard longitudinal analysis by employing a continuous multi-state modeling framework. The system actively tracks all participants, including those who disengage from clinical care, through periodic direct contact and mandatory linkage to national death and hospitalization registries to capture critical endpoints. This design explicitly models competing risks and informative censoring, allowing for the estimation of unbiased transition probabilities between glycemic control states and complication onset. The result is a robust, real-world prediction model for complication risk that accounts for the full spectrum of patient outcomes, including death and care discontinuation.

ideas.title: NLP-Extracted Latent Complications for Predictive Glycemic Deterioration Modeling
ideas.description: This research applies advanced NLP to unstructured clinician notes to extract subtle mentions of potential complications (e.g., minor neuropathic pain, fleeting visual changes) and social determinants of health (e.g., food insecurity, stress events) that are not captured in structured data. These NLP-derived 'latent complication signals' are then used as time-stamped inputs to a longitudinal inverse problem model designed to determine if, and how strongly, these early, often undocumented signs predict a subsequent statistically significant deterioration in glycemic control (e.g., rising HbA1c, increased variability). The novel synergy lies in creating a predictive pipeline that identifies at-risk patients not by known, recorded complications, but by the narrative precursors to those complications, thereby shifting prediction from a reactive to a truly pre-emptive paradigm.

ideas.description: This research adapts the high-frequency, in-situ tracking approach from behavioral ecology (e.g., Ecological Momentary Assessment) to the problem of linking attitudes with titration behavior. Instead of relying solely on infrequent clinic visits, patients and their HCPs use a study app to complete brief, context-triggered surveys about attitudes, intentions, and decisional factors immediately before and after titration events. This dense, longitudinal data on the 'micro-decisions' and attitudes is temporally linked to objective, continuous glycemic data (from CGMs) and traditional longitudinal outcomes (HbA1c). This method captures the dynamic interplay between psychological factors and behavior as it unfolds, providing a more precise causal map of what drives adherence and subsequent glycemic outcomes than retrospective or low-frequency surveys alone.

ideas.title: Modeling the Economic Impact of Clinical Inertia in BI as a Corrosion Prevention Problem
ideas.description: This research adapts the life-cycle cost analysis (LCCA) framework from materials science and corrosion engineering to quantify the economic impact of clinical inertia in type 2 diabetes management. The analogous problem is preventing material degradation: just as unchecked corrosion leads to exponentially higher repair or replacement costs, clinical inertia (delayed treatment titration) leads to preventable complications and far higher long-term healthcare costs. The solution is to model the 'do-nothing' scenario's total cost trajectory against the upfront and operational costs of interventions designed to promote timely titration (e.g., digital alerts, dedicated titration clinics). The adapted research will develop a simulation model that treats clinical inertia as a corrosive process on population health, identifying the break-even points and optimal investment levels for anti-inertia interventions to minimize the total life-cycle cost of diabetes care for a healthcare payer.

ideas.description: This redesigned approach eliminates the arbitrary ≥24-month disease duration requirement by implementing an individualized stability verification phase. All prospective participants undergo a high-frequency monitoring run-in period using digital and clinical tools to establish a personal disease trajectory baseline. Eligibility is then determined by a statistical model that confirms the absence of a significant worsening trend against this individual baseline, rather than relying on time since diagnosis. This method ensures the enrolled cohort has verifiably stable disease while enabling the earlier inclusion of patients whose condition plateaus rapidly, thereby increasing recruitment efficiency and scientific precision.

ideas.title: Co-Designing Patient-Activated EHR Data Capture to Improve Complication Monitoring and Titration Adherence

First, it uses insights from patient surveys to educate users on specific complications (like neuropathy) and address fears about medication titration. Second, it actively solicits structured, patient-reported symptom data on those same complications, creating a parallel data stream to validate and supplement potentially undercoded EHR entries. By analyzing discrepancies between patient-reported outcomes and clinician-coded complications, the tool can identify systemic gaps in EHR data capture. This enriched, patient-validated dataset then directly feeds back to personalize and improve the titration decision aids, creating a more responsive and evidence-based support system.

ideas.title: Age-stratified validation of self-reported clinical behavior against objective records
ideas.description: This research investigates the interplay between age heterogeneity and the accuracy of self-reported clinical behavior. It tests the hypothesis that the correlation between healthcare providers' or patients' stated attitudes (e.g., willingness to titrate medication) and their objectively measured actions in clinical records is not uniform across age groups. By stratifying analyses by finer age categories or risk profiles within traditional decades, the study can identify specific patient subgroups where self-reports are strong predictors of real-world behavior and others where they are highly discordant. This enables more precise targeting of interventions to improve care, focusing on gaps where perception most misaligns with practice.

ideas.title: Adaptive Patient Titration Platform with Continuous Feedback Integration
ideas.description: This research develops a dynamic digital platform for medication titration that moves beyond static educational aids. The platform delivers personalized support by integrating initial survey insights with real-time data on individual patient interactions, reported outcomes, and barriers. A backend system continuously analyzes this usage data alongside monitored clinical guidelines and public health discourse to identify emerging knowledge gaps or new misconceptions. This enables the platform's educational content and decision-support logic to be automatically updated and tailored, creating a learning system that adapts to both population-level trends and individual patient needs over time.
